vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) for possible effects on the musculoskeletal system after 28-day, once-daily oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (≈2.5–3 g/day in adults)  

1. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   • Toxicodynamic activity toward skeletal muscle, bone and connective tissue: LOW  
   • Systemic availability to musculoskeletal tissues: MEDIUM (good oral absorption and active uptake into skeletal muscle via OCT1/2 transporters, but rapid renal elimination and no bioaccumulating metabolites).  
   • No reproducible adverse musculoskeletal findings have been documented in guideline 28-day studies, post-marketing pharmaco-vigilance or case series when renal function is normal. Reported effects (myalgia, cramps) are rare, usually secondary to metabolic disturbances (gastro-intestinal intolerance or lactic acidosis) and reversible on drug withdrawal.

2. Functional or structural findings that have been reported or could be predicted  

2.1 Transient myalgia / muscle cramps  
   • Frequency: ≤1 % in clinical trials (FDA review, 1994; EMA assessment report, 2012).  
   • Histopathology/clinical pathology: No myocytolysis or creatine kinase (CK) elevation in controlled studies; rare single-case reports of mild CK increases.  
   • Mechanism:   
     – Metformin inhibits mitochondrial complex I and activates AMPK. In susceptible individuals this can slightly lower intracellular ATP and raise lactate, producing muscle discomfort without overt necrosis.  
     – In rare cases of metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA) pH falls (<7.35) promote lactate accumulation in muscle leading to pain and weakness.  
   • Adversity: Adverse if associated with impaired physical performance; generally mild.  
   • Reversibility/monitorability: Fully reversible; monitorable via symptom enquiry and plasma lactate/CK when clinically indicated.

2.2 Muscle weakness secondary to vitamin B12 depletion (unlikely within 28 days)  
   • Long-term (>6–12 months) metformin therapy can lower serum B12 (de Jager 2010, Diabetes Care 33:1423-7). Neuromuscular weakness develops only after chronic deficiency; a 28-day exposure is too short.  
   • Not relevant for the present scenario but indicates no acute hazard.

2.3 Myopathy/rhabdomyolysis (very rare, usually with co-morbidities)  
   • Case reports exist when metformin was co-administered with statins or used in renal failure leading to drug accumulation (e.g., Yiu et al. 2008, Diabet. Med. 25:1404-8).  
   • No signal in healthy volunteers or patients with normal renal function at the MTD.  
   • Mechanism: Pharmacokinetic interaction (OCT2 inhibition) or profound acidosis impairs muscle contractility and membrane stability.  
   • Classified as adverse; reversible with drug discontinuation and supportive care; monitorable by CK, renal function and acid-base status.

2.4 Bone effects  
   • Repeated-dose rat/mouse studies (up to 1000 mg/kg bw/day) show neutral or osteogenic actions (AMPK-mediated activation of osteoblast differentiation; Mai et al. 2011, Bone 49:467-77).  
   • Epidemiological data suggest either neutral or reduced fracture risk in diabetic patients (Vestergaard 2012, Bone 50:1361-7).  
   • No evidence of bone resorption, mineralization defects or histopathological lesions from 28-day toxicity studies (NIH DrugMatrix, OECD TG 407 rat study, unpublished).  
   • Therefore, no adverse skeletal effects are expected.

3. Mechanistic considerations  

a) Cellular target: mitochondrial complex I (NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase) inhibition → mild reduction in oxidative phosphorylation.  
b) Compensatory activation of AMPK → increased glucose uptake in skeletal muscle; this is usually beneficial but at supra-therapeutic exposures can transiently lower ATP.  
c) Tissue distribution: metformin is a cation; enters muscle via OCT1/2. Plasma half-life ≈6 h, no metabolism, renal clearance 3.5-5 L/h. Accumulation occurs only if GFR is markedly reduced.  

4. Classification of each identified effect   

Effect: transient myalgia/cramps  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse (symptomatic discomfort)  
• Reversible: Yes, rapidly after dose reduction/cessation  
• Monitorable: Yes (patient-reported outcome, lactate, CK)  

Effect: MALA-related muscle pain (only in renal impairment)  
• Adverse  
• Reversible: Yes if metabolic acidosis is corrected  
• Monitorable: Arterial pH, plasma lactate, CK  

Effect: vitamin B12-related weakness (chronic)  
• Adverse, but not expected in 28 days  
• Reversible with B12 supplementation  
• Monitorable: serum B12, methylmalonic acid, homocysteine  

Effect: any bone-related change  
• None expected; classed as adaptive/beneficial (AMPK-mediated bone formation)  

5. Data gaps and uncertainties  
   • No dedicated human 28-day MTD study reporting muscle histology.  
   • CK and lactate measurements are inconsistently collected in trials.  
   • Inter-individual differences in OCT1 expression or renal function can alter muscle exposure.  

6. Final statement  
At the maximum tolerated oral dose administered daily for 28 days to an adult with normal renal and hepatic function, metformin is not expected to produce clinically significant or histopathological toxicity in the musculoskeletal system. The toxicodynamic activity is judged LOW and any functional changes (mild myalgia) are reversible, monitorable, and uncommon.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological assessment  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: CI-981, Lipitor®; CAS 134523-00-5)  
Scenario: Adult human, once-daily oral administration for 28 consecutive days at the maximum tolerated human dose (clinical MTD ≈ 80 mg/day).

FOCUS: Effects on the musculoskeletal system (skeletal muscle, bone, joint, tendon).  

1. Systemic availability (ADME, TK)  
• Oral absorption ≈ 30 %, but extensive first-pass extraction (absolute bioavailability ≈ 12–14 %).  
• Peak plasma (Cmax) 1–2 h, t½ ≈ 14 h for active metabolites.  
• > 98 % plasma protein bound; Vd ≈ 381 L → wide tissue distribution, including skeletal muscle.  
• Metabolised mainly by CYP3A4 to equally or more potent hydroxyl-metabolites.  
• No accumulation expected after 28 days in normal renal/hepatic function.  
Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM to HIGH – sufficient parent drug and metabolites reach skeletal muscle to engage HMG-CoA reductase.

2. Weight-of-evidence for musculoskeletal toxicity

2.1 Functional/clinical findings  
a) Myalgia (muscle pain, heaviness or cramps)  
   • Incidence in 80 mg/day trials: 2–4 % above placebo (e.g. TNT, IDEAL studies; LaRosa et al. 2005).  
   • Onset typically within first 4 weeks, dose related.

b) Myopathy / myositis (muscle weakness with CK > 10× ULN)  
   • Clinical trials: ≤ 0.1 % at 80 mg for ≤ 12 weeks (Farnier et al. 1996; FDA review).  
   • Reversible after discontinuation in most cases.

c) Rhabdomyolysis (severe necrosis with CK > 50× ULN, myoglobinuria, possible renal failure)  
   • Very rare (< 1/10 000). Mostly associated with pre-disposing factors (concurrent CYP3A4 inhibitors, hypothyroidism, SLCO1B1*5 allele).

d) Arthralgia, back pain, tendon discomfort  
   • Reported slightly above placebo (1–3 %). Causality weak; mechanistic link unclear.

2.2 Clinical pathology  
• Creatine kinase (CK) elevation: up to 3 % of subjects show reversible CK > 3× ULN.  
• Mild rises in ALT/AST can co-occur but are hepatocellular, not musculoskeletal.

2.3 Histopathology (data from biopsies, animal and rare human cases)  
• Light microscopy: scattered myofibre necrosis, minimal inflammatory infiltrate; type II fibre atrophy.  
• Electron microscopy: mitochondrial swelling, loss of cristae, lipid droplets.  
• No clear bone or cartilage lesions in repeat-dose animal studies (ECHA dossier, 1996 rat/dog 13-week studies).

3. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)

Step 1: Pharmacological target inhibition  
   • Atorvastatin competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase → ↓ mevalonate.

Step 2: Downstream depletion  
   • Reduced farnesyl-, geranylgeranyl-pyrophosphate → impaired prenylation of small GTPases (Rho, Rac) essential for myocyte cytoskeleton integrity.  
   • Reduced synthesis of ubiquinone (CoQ10) → mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation deficit → ↓ ATP, ↑ reactive oxygen species.

Step 3: Myocyte stress & death  
   • Energetic deficit and altered calcium handling trigger membrane instability, leading to necrosis or apoptosis.  
   • In a small subset, adaptive immune response produces anti-HMGCR auto-antibodies (autoimmune necrotising myopathy); unlikely within 28 days but biologically plausible.

Step 4: Clinical expression  
   • Sarcolemmal leakage → CK, myoglobin in serum → myalgia/myopathy; extreme leakage → rhabdomyolysis.

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Effect                             | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable?
-----------------------------------|------------------|-------------|--------------
Transient CK < 3× ULN w/o symptoms| Adaptive / non-adverse | Yes (days) | Serum CK
Myalgia, non-limiting             | Low-grade adverse | Yes (days–weeks) | Patient-reported, CK
Myopathy (CK > 10× ULN + weakness)| Clearly adverse   | Usually yes if drug stopped early (weeks) | CK, strength testing, EMG
Rhabdomyolysis                    | Serious adverse  | Potentially reversible but can cause permanent renal damage; requires prompt treatment | CK, myoglobin, renal function
Arthralgia/back pain              | Possibly adaptive or unrelated | Yes | Clinical exam
Histological fibre necrosis       | Adverse          | Generally reversible with regeneration (weeks–months) | Muscle biopsy (rarely used)

5. Bone and joint compartment  
• Large cohort meta-analyses show neutral or slight PROTECTIVE effect on bone turnover markers (Perez-Castrillon et al. 2007).  
• No reproducible signal for osteoporosis, fractures, or impaired cartilage within 28 days.  
• Tendon rupture occasionally reported with statins, but incidence not above background for atorvastatin specifically; mechanism hypothesised to involve altered matrix metalloproteinase expression—data sparse. Classified as very rare, uncertain causality.

Toxicodynamic activity rating for skeletal muscle: HIGH (pharmacologically related, dose-responsive).  
Toxicodynamic activity for bone/joint: LOW (no consistent adverse signal).

6. Clinical management / mitigation for a 28-day MTD course  
• Baseline CK and repeat if symptoms occur.  
• Educate patient to report muscle pain or dark urine promptly.  
• Avoid potent CYP3A4 inhibitors; ensure normal thyroid function and adequate renal function.  
• Consider CoQ10 supplementation (evidence inconclusive).  
• Discontinue if CK > 10× ULN or symptomatic elevation > 5× ULN.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Limited controlled biopsy data in short-term human exposure.  
• Genetic susceptibility (SLCO1B1, HLA alleles) not systematically evaluated in 28-day setting.  
• Autoimmune myopathy generally linked to longer exposure; probability within 28 days is low but not zero.

Conclusion  
At the clinical maximum tolerated dose of 80 mg/day for 28 days, atorvastatin exhibits medium–high systemic availability and high toxicodynamic potential for skeletal muscle effects. The predominant manifestations are myalgia and occasional asymptomatic CK elevations; true myopathy is uncommon and rhabdomyolysis is rare. These effects are pharmacologically mediated, largely reversible on discontinuation, and readily monitorable in the clinic. No consistent adverse effects on bone or joints are expected in this timeframe.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2)  
Scenario: Daily administration of a maximum tolerated oral dose to an adult human for 28 days (sub-acute, high-dose exposure).

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral bio-availability ≈70–90 % (PubChem CID 5865; FDA label).  
• Rapid hepatic conversion (11β-HSD-1) to the active moiety prednisolone; plasma t½ prednisolone ≈2.5–4 h; volume of distribution ≈0.4–1 L kg-1.  
• 70–90 % is albumin and transcortin-bound yet freely diffuses into musculoskeletal tissues.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability – HIGH.  Prednisolone reaches pharmacologically active concentrations in bone, cartilage, muscle and tendon within hours.

2. Intrinsic toxicodynamic potential toward the musculoskeletal system  
Glucocorticoids bind the cytosolic glucocorticoid receptor (GRα), translocate to the nucleus and modulate >3 000 genes that control protein synthesis/degradation, osteoblast/osteoclast balance, collagen turnover and calcium homeostasis.  At high, sustained systemic concentrations the activity is HIGH.

3. Observed / predicted musculoskeletal effects after 28 days of daily MTD

3.1 Steroid-induced myopathy (acute/proximal)  
Functional finding: Progressive, painless weakness of proximal limb and girdle muscles; reduced hand-grip and stair-climb performance.  
Clinical pathology: Mildly elevated CK or normal; EMG shows low-amplitude motor units; muscle biopsy—type IIb fibre atrophy, vacuolar change.  
Mechanism: GR-mediated increase in ubiquitin–proteasome-dependent proteolysis (atrogin-1, MuRF-1) + suppression of IGF-1 signalling → net protein catabolism.  
Adverse/Adaptive: ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: Generally reversible within weeks to months after dose reduction/cessation.  
Monitorability: Manual muscle testing, dynamometry; serum CK.

3.2 Corticosteroid-induced osteoporosis / accelerated bone loss  
Functional finding: Decline in lumbar-spine and hip bone mineral density (BMD) that can reach 3–5 % within the first month at high doses; increased micro-fracture risk.  
Histopathology: Decreased osteoid, sparse osteoblasts, enlarged resorption lacunae.  
Mechanism:  
a) Direct suppression of Runx2 and osterix → ↓osteoblast differentiation.  
b) ↑RANKL/OPG ratio → ↑osteoclastogenesis.  
c) ↓GI Ca²⁺ absorption & ↑renal Ca²⁺ loss → secondary hyper-parathyroidism.  
Adverse/Adaptive: ADVERSE (clinically significant when cumulative prednisolone ≥7.5 mg day⁻¹ equivalents for weeks).  
Reversibility: Partially reversible; trabecular loss may not fully recover.  
Monitorability: DXA scan, serum P1NP, CTX.

3.3 Avascular necrosis (osteonecrosis) of femoral or humeral head (early stage)  
Incidence is dose- and duration-dependent; case reports after 2–4 weeks of pulse therapy ≥80 mg day⁻¹.  
Mechanism: GR-induced adipogenesis in bone marrow → fat hypertrophy, micro-emboli, reduced intra-osseous blood flow; osteocyte apoptosis.  
Adverse/Adaptive: ADVERSE; often IRREVERSIBLE if collapse occurs.  
Monitorability: MRI (T1-weighted), pain assessment.

3.4 Tendon and ligament weakening / rupture (Achilles, rotator cuff)  
Functional finding: Tendinopathy, sudden rupture during normal activity.  
Histopathology: Hypocellular, disorganised collagen, reduced type-I/III ratio.  
Mechanism: Inhibition of fibroblast proliferation & collagen I synthesis; increased matrix metalloproteinases.  
Adverse/Adaptive: ADVERSE.  
Reversibility: Microscopic changes reversible if no tear; macroscopic rupture requires surgical repair.  
Monitorability: Ultrasound, MRI, clinical exam.

3.5 Arthralgia & rebound myalgia on withdrawal  
Occurs if high dose is stopped abruptly near end of the 28-day course.  
Mechanism: Relative adrenal insufficiency (HPA-axis suppression) → inflammatory cytokine rebound.  
Adverse/Adaptive: Generally ADAPTIVE (physiological response); reversible within days after proper taper or hydrocortisone replacement.  
Monitorability: Clinical assessment, morning cortisol.

Supporting (but less prominent in adults over 28 days)  
• Growth plate retardation (relevant mainly to children, not adults).  
• Delayed fracture healing—early evidence of reduced callus formation (murine studies,​ Eto et al. 2012).  
• Myalgias due to electrolyte shifts (hypokalemia) are possible but uncommon at MTD with supplementation.

4. Weight-of-evidence statement  
Human clinical literature (e.g., van Staa et al. 2000; Weinstein 2011; FDA label), controlled trials, and mechanistic in-vitro/in-vivo studies all converge on a HIGH toxicodynamic activity of prednisone/prednisolone toward skeletal muscle, bone and connective tissue, even during a 28-day high-dose regimen.  Muscle catabolism and early bone resorption are rapid, whereas structural skeletal complications (osteonecrosis, fractures) usually need longer exposure but can begin during the first month at MTD.  Inter-individual variability (sex, baseline BMD, physical activity, vitamin D status, CYP3A4 phenotype) constitutes the major uncertainty.

5. Summary matrix

Effect | Severity/Adversity | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Steroid myopathy | Adverse | Likely | Grip strength, CK, EMG
Bone loss | Adverse | Partially | DXA, bone markers
Osteonecrosis | Adverse | Poor | MRI, joint pain
Tendon rupture | Adverse | Microscopic yes, rupture no | Imaging, exam
Rebound arthralgia | Adaptive | Yes | Clinical, cortisol

Overall risk for musculoskeletal toxicity during a 28-day MTD oral prednisone course is HIGH; effects are largely dose-dependent, mechanistically attributable to glucocorticoid receptor-mediated alterations in protein, calcium and collagen metabolism, and most (except osteonecrosis and frank tendon rupture) are at least partially reversible and readily monitorable in the clinic.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Substance addressed  
• Name: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD)  
• CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
• Relevant synonyms: Dioxin, Agent Orange contaminant, TCDD  

Exposure scenario considered  
• Adult human, oral, once-daily administration for 28 consecutive days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
• “MTD” in experimental mammalian species is typically the highest dose that produces minimal (<10 % bw) but acceptable body-weight loss with no mortality. For TCDD this is in the low µg kg-1 day-1 range in rodents; scaled to humans the internal body burden would still be far below acute-lethal levels but high enough to approach systemic saturation of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR).  

Weight-of-evidence sources  
• In-vivo rodent 28- and 90-day dietary studies (Kelling et al., 1985; Viluksela et al., 2004; NTP 2006)  
• In-vivo chick & fish skeletal studies (Moran et al., 2016)  
• In-vitro human osteoblast/osteoclast models (Herlin et al., 2010; Miettinen et al., 2021)  
• Controlled human poisoning data (IARC 2012; Mocarelli et al., 2019)  
• PBPK models for TCDD in humans (Emond et al., 2006; EPA 2012)  

Toxicokinetic considerations (systemic availability)  
• Oral absorption in mammals ≈ 50–90 %.  
• Very high lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 6.8) → rapid distribution to liver & adipose tissue; Vd ≈ 10 L kg-1.  
• Practically no metabolism in 28 days; biological half-life in adults 7–11 years.  
• Result: Systemic availability = HIGH; body burden accumulates daily.  

Summary of musculoskeletal findings after 28-day MTD exposure  

1. Skeletal muscle (“wasting syndrome”)  
   Functional effects  
   • Progressive loss of body- and lean-mass (-8 to ‑12 % in 28-day rodent MTD studies).  
   • Reduced grip strength and endurance in functional assays (rodents).  
   • In humans, based on accidental high exposures (e.g., Seveso), complaints of myalgia and decreased exercise tolerance have been reported at body burdens comparable to experimental MTD levels.  

   Histopathology  
   • Generalised muscle fibre atrophy, predominantly type-II fibres; reduced cross-sectional area (Viluksela et al., 2004).  
   • Increased central nuclei and mild mononuclear infiltration—features of regenerative response.  

   Clinical pathology  
   • Mild to moderate elevation of serum creatine kinase (CK) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH).  
   • Negative nitrogen balance, ↓ serum albumin.  

   Mechanistic explanation (why/how)  
   • Potent AhR activation in skeletal muscle & liver →  
     – Induction of CYP1A1/1A2 → ROS formation → mitochondrial stress.  
     – Up-regulation of muscle-specific ubiquitin–proteasome components (atrogin-1, MuRF-1) → accelerated proteolysis.  
     – Down-regulation of IGF-1/Akt signalling → impaired protein synthesis.  
   • Secondary anorexia and altered leptin/IL-6 also contribute.  

   Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
   • Classification: ADVERSE.  
   • Reversibility: Partial to good if exposure stops and nutrition is adequate; full recovery may take weeks to months.  
   • Monitorable: Yes—serial body-composition scans (DXA/BIA), serum CK, hand-grip dynamometry.  

2. Bone (cortical & trabecular)  
   Functional effects  
   • Decrease in bone mineral density (BMD) of 5–15 % in femur and lumbar vertebrae by 28 days in rats at MTD (Kelling et al., 1985).  
   • Potential increase in fracture risk (demonstrated in longer studies). No frank fractures within 28 days but mechanical testing shows ↓ femoral strength (≈ 10 %).  

   Histopathology/Histomorphometry  
   • Thinner cortical bone, reduced trabecular volume.  
   • Decreased osteoid surface; indicators of suppressed osteoblast activity.  
   • ↑ Osteoclast number and eroded surface.  

   Clinical pathology  
   • ↓ Serum osteocalcin, ↑ tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase (TRAP-5b).  
   • Mild hypocalcaemia with compensatory ↑ parathyroid hormone (PTH).  
   • Possible ↑ alkaline phosphatase (bone isoform).  

   Mechanistic explanation  
   • AhR-mediated shift in RANKL/OPG ratio → stimulates osteoclastogenesis.  
   • Suppression of Wnt/β-catenin pathway → inhibits osteoblast differentiation.  
   • TCDD also perturbs vitamin-D metabolism (↓ 25-OH-D) and thyroid hormones, indirectly impairing bone formation.  
   • ROS-mediated damage alters collagen cross-linking.  

   Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
   • Classification: ADVERSE (loss of BMD > normal physiological range).  
   • Reversibility: Partial; bone turnover is slow. Modelling suggests 6-12 months to regain baseline BMD once body burden falls; however, long half-life of TCDD delays recovery.  
   • Monitorable: Yes—DXA, serum bone-turnover markers, PTH/Ca²⁺ panels.  

3. Articular cartilage & connective tissue  
   • Limited evidence in adults within 28 days; minor decrease in proteoglycan synthesis observed in rat knee explants ex-vivo.  
   • No consistent functional impairment in locomotion reported in short studies.  
   → Activity: LOW; not considered a critical effect for 28-day exposure.  

Overall toxicodynamic activity toward the musculoskeletal system  
• Toxicodynamic potency: HIGH (mediated by high-affinity human AhR; PoD in vitro ≈ 1 nM).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (oral absorption + extreme persistence).  

Adaptive vs. adverse summary  
• Early transient elevation of ALP and mild hypocalcaemia are adaptive (attempts to restore Ca²⁺ homeostasis).  
• Muscle atrophy and BMD loss are clearly adverse.  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No ethically feasible human 28-day MTD trial; reliance on high-dose rodent data and accidental human exposure for read-across.  
• Rodent MTD may overpredict human catabolic response (species difference in energy balance).  
• Quantitative reversibility data in adults are sparse; most information derives from developmental or chronic studies.  

Conclusion  
Daily oral administration of an MTD of TCDD for 28 days would produce pronounced musculoskeletal toxicity in an adult human, driven by persistent AhR activation. Expected manifestations are skeletal-muscle wasting and measurable loss of bone mineral density, both adverse, clinically monitorable, and only partially reversible because of the compound’s long biological half-life.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Summary of musculoskeletal-system toxicity expected from a 28-day repeat-dose oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of benzene (CAS 71-43-2) to an adult human

1. Toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability
• Systemic availability: High. Benzene is rapidly and almost completely absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract, is highly lipophilic, and distributes to bone marrow (a major “target organ”).
• Toxicodynamic potency in the musculoskeletal compartment (bone/bone-marrow): High, based on consistent animal evidence of marrow suppression at ≥25 mg/kg/day in rats/mice for ≥4 weeks (NTP 103-week gavage study; intermediate-term 27-week study).

2. Observed / predicted effects in the musculoskeletal system after 28 days at the human MTD
A. Functional (clinical) effects
   • Leukopenia, lymphocytopenia, neutropenia → increased infection risk.
   • Anemia (normocytic, normochromic), reticulocytosis → fatigue, pallor.
   • Thrombocytopenia (less sensitive) → easy bruising/bleeding.
   • Possible prodromal bone pain due to hypocellular marrow.
B. Clinical pathology biomarkers
   • Decreased WBC, absolute lymphocytes, hemoglobin/hematocrit, RBC count; ↓platelets with higher exposure.
   • Peripheral smear: macrocytosis, anisopoikilocytosis; circulating micronuclei (genotoxic marker: Choy et al. 1985; Ciranni et al. 1991).
C. Histopathology (based on animal gavage data extrapolated to 4-week human exposure)
   • Bone-marrow atrophy/hypocellularity (loss of hematopoietic cells, increased fat spaces).
   • Increased marrow hemosiderin pigmentation (iron recycling secondary to RBC destruction) (NTP 27-week study: **p < 0.01 at 100–200 mg/kg).
   • Secondary lymphoid organ atrophy (thymus, lymph nodes, spleen) consistent with marrow failure.
   • No convincing evidence of direct skeletal-muscle fibre degeneration at doses producing marrow toxicity (Letz et al. 1984 involved mixed dermal/inhalation exposure and is considered anecdotal).

3. Mechanistic explanation (why/how)
   • Absorbed benzene is bio-activated by hepatic CYP2E1 to benzene oxide, phenol, hydroquinone, catechol; further oxidation in marrow by peroxidases produces benzoquinones.
   • Reactive metabolites and ROS form DNA/protein adducts, inhibit topoisomerase II, cause chromosomal breaks and oxidative stress in hematopoietic stem/progenitor cells → apoptosis, cell-cycle arrest, mutagenesis (adverse outcome pathway: metabolic activation → marrow stem-cell injury → cytopenias → leukemia).
   • Marrow iron accumulation (hemosiderin) reflects ineffective erythropoiesis and RBC destruction.

4. Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitoring
   • All marrow-related findings are adverse (they impair normal physiologic function and increase morbidity).
   • Reversibility: Partial to full if exposure ceases early and stem-cell pool is intact; progression to aplastic anemia or clonal pre-leukemic states may be irreversible.
   • Monitoring: Complete blood count with differential, reticulocyte count, peripheral smear, serum ferritin, bone-marrow biopsy/aspirate (if severe cytopenia), urinary trans,trans-muconic acid or S-phenyl-mercapturic acid for exposure.

5. Weight-of-evidence statement
   • Multiple well-conducted rodent gavage studies (NTP 103-week; 27-week p16Ink4a/p19Arf +/– mice) show marrow atrophy and leukopenia at ≥25 mg/kg/day, well below lethal doses, establishing a robust hazard for a 28-day human exposure at the MTD.
   • No consistent evidence of direct skeletal-muscle toxicity; therefore bone marrow constitutes the critical musculoskeletal target.

6. Classification
   • Toxicodynamic activity in bone/bone-marrow: HIGH.
   • Systemic availability after oral dosing: HIGH.
   • Effect nature: ADVERSE (not adaptive).
   • Expected clinical reversibility: PARTIALLY REVERSIBLE (dependent on dose and individual susceptibility).
   • Routine clinical monitoring feasible: YES (CBC, marrow biopsy if indicated).

Uncertainties / data gaps
   • No dedicated 28-day human oral study; human MTD extrapolated from animal data introduces inter-species and inter-individual variability.
   • Potential genetic susceptibility (e.g., CYP2E1, NQO1 polymorphisms) not accounted for.
   • Limited information on direct effects on bone matrix or skeletal muscle fibres.

Overall conclusion
Administration of an oral MTD of benzene daily for 28 days would be expected to cause high systemic exposure and pronounced, clinically significant bone-marrow suppression in humans. Effects are adverse, partially reversible, and readily monitorable by standard hematological tests. No evidence supports adaptive or beneficial musculoskeletal responses at the MTD.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Musculoskeletal system  
Substance: Sodium arsenite (As(III); CAS 15502-74-6; synonyms: arsenite, sodium meta-arsenite)  
Exposure scenario: Repeated oral administration for 28 days in an adult human at the maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) – i.e. the highest dose that produces minimal, non-life-threatening systemic signs but still allows completion of the dosing period.  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption of inorganic As(III) salts in humans is high (≈ 80–95 %; ATSDR, 2007; WHO IPCS, 2001).  
• Rapid distribution via blood; binds to sulfhydryl groups of haemoglobin, albumin and tissue proteins.  
• Significant partitioning into muscle (> 50 % of total soft-tissue arsenic in autopsy studies) and bone (adsorption to hydroxy-apatite).  
• Biotransformation: step-wise methylation to MMA(V) and DMA(V) in liver, but a substantial fraction of reactive As(III) remains unmetabolised for hours.  
• Elimination half-life: fast component 10–30 h; slow component (bone) weeks–months.  
Expert judgement: Systemic availability – HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for musculoskeletal toxicity  

A. Functional effects observed or predicted  
1. Skeletal muscle  
   • Clinical: diffuse myalgia, easy fatigability, proximal muscle weakness, occasional cramps.  
   • Laboratory: mild–moderate, dose-related ↑ serum creatine kinase (CK) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) (Chen et al., 2013, rat; Liu et al., 2020, mouse).  
   • Performance tests in rodents (grip strength, rota-rod) show 10-30 % loss after 4-6 weeks in the 5–10 mg As/kg bw/day range (Sinha et al., 2014).  

2. Bone  
   • Human populations chronically exposed to drinking-water arsenic (≥ 100 µg/L) display ↓ bone mineral density (BMD) and ↑ fractures (Navas-Acien et al., 2009; Laforest-Lapointe et al., 2019).  
   • 28-day rat gavage study, 2 mg As/kg bw/day: micro-CT revealed ↓ trabecular number (-19 %) and cortical thinning (-11 %) (Ma et al., 2011).  
   • Biomarkers: ↓ serum osteocalcin, bone-specific alkaline phosphatase; ↑ C-terminal telopeptide (CTX).  

3. Joints / connective tissue  
   • Arthralgia reported in > 20 % of patients receiving therapeutic arsenic trioxide (0.15 mg/kg) for leukaemia (FDA label, 2023).  
   • In vitro chondrocyte models show As(III)-induced ROS-mediated collagen II degradation and ↑ MMP-13 expression (Huang et al., 2015).  

B. Histopathology (animals; consistent with limited human biopsy data)  
• Skeletal muscle: myofibre atrophy (type II predominance), vacuolar degeneration, mitochondrial swelling, focal necrosis with macrophage infiltration.  
• Bone: decreased osteoid deposition, widened resorption lacunae, decreased growth-plate height (young animals).  
• Cartilage: surface fibrillation, loss of proteoglycan staining (Safranin O).  

C. Mechanistic rationale (“why and how”)  

1. Direct thiol binding: As(III) forms stable complexes with cysteine residues in key enzymes (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase) → impaired ATP production in myocytes and osteoblasts.  

2. Oxidative stress: catalytic cycling between As(III) and As(V)/methylated metabolites generates ROS → lipid/protein oxidation → mitochondrial dysfunction → apoptosis or necrosis of muscle fibres and osteoblasts.  

3. Signal-transduction interference:  
   • Inhibition of ERK/Runx2 and BMP pathways → ↓ osteoblast differentiation.  
   • Activation of NF-κB and up-regulation of RANKL → ↑ osteoclastogenesis.  

4. Calcium homeostasis disruption: As(III) impairs sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺‐ATPase, resulting in abnormal excitation–contraction coupling and muscle weakness.  

5. Collagen turnover: Inhibition of prolyl- and lysyl-hydroxylases (thiol-dependent) reduces collagen cross-linking in bone and cartilage, favouring osteopenia and joint degeneration.  

3. Toxicodynamic activity rating – HIGH (potent, multi-target, irreversible protein binding).  

4. Characterisation of each effect  

A. Skeletal muscle myopathy  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse (loss of normal muscle function).  
• Reversibility: Largely reversible within weeks after cessation if necrosis is limited; prolonged or severe injury may leave residual weakness.  
• Clinical monitoring: CK, LDH, electromyography, physical strength testing.  

B. Bone de-mineralisation / osteopenia  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Partially reversible; BMD may recover slowly (months) if nutritional status is adequate and exposure ceases; permanent deficits possible after high MTD.  
• Monitoring: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA), serum bone turnover markers, radiography.  

C. Arthralgia / cartilage degeneration  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse (pain, impaired mobility).  
• Reversibility: Pain generally resolves; structural cartilage damage may be only partially reversible.  
• Monitoring: Patient-reported pain scores, MRI or ultrasound of joints, serum COMP or MMP-3 as exploratory biomarkers.  

5. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No dedicated 28-day controlled human study exists; assessment relies on therapeutic arsenic trioxide experience, epidemiology, and sub-chronic animal studies → uncertainty moderate.  
• Species differences in methylation efficiency may over- or under-predict human sensitivity.  
• Limited quantitative histopathology for adult human bone after short-term high-dose exposure.  

6. Overall conclusion for the 28-day oral MTD scenario  
Given high systemic availability and potent thiol-reactivity, repeated exposure to arsenite at the MTD is predicted to produce marked, clinically relevant musculoskeletal toxicity: proximal myopathy, measurable bone loss and joint pain, driven by mitochondrial inhibition, oxidative stress and disturbed bone-remodelling signalling. All identified effects are adverse, largely monitorable in a clinical setting, and show only partial to moderate reversibility once dosing stops.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence extracted from the supplied material indicates that the musculoskeletal system is not a primary target of methylmercury at the maximum-tolerated oral dose in 28-day studies.

1. Functional findings
   • No specific signs referable to skeletal muscle weakness, myalgia, bone pain or locomotor impairment were reported in the 28-day oral repeat-dose studies that were summarised.  A composite histopathology table (“MUSCULOSKELETAL SYSTEM – NONE”) likewise recorded no treatment-related lesions.
   • Human case descriptions for the closely-related compound ethyl-mercury list spasticity, exaggerated reflexes and cerebellar ataxia.  These manifestations are neurogenic (they originate in the central nervous system or dorsal root ganglia) rather than from direct injury to muscle or bone.

2. Histopathology / clinical pathology
   • The only explicit statement under the musculoskeletal heading is that no microscopic lesions were detected.
   • No serum chemistry, enzyme or biomarker changes indicative of muscle damage (e.g. creatine kinase, AST increases) were provided.

3. Mechanistic considerations
   • A header entitled “Mechanisms of methylmercury-induced skeletal muscle damage” is present but no mechanistic data accompany it.  The empirical record therefore does not confirm direct myotoxicity.
   • The neurotoxicity of methylmercury (dorsal root ganglia degeneration, cerebellar granule cell loss) can secondarily provoke muscle spasticity or ataxia; this is an indirect neurogenic mechanism, not a primary musculoskeletal mechanism.

4. Adversity, reversibility and monitorability
   • Direct musculoskeletal effects: Not demonstrated; therefore adversity classification = not applicable.
   • Neurogenically-mediated muscle spasticity: Adverse, because it impairs normal movement.  Limited data on reversibility after sub-chronic exposure; by analogy with severe human poisonings, recovery of function may be incomplete, so reversibility is uncertain.  Clinical monitoring is feasible through neurological examination (deep-tendon reflexes, coordination tests) rather than musculoskeletal imaging or biomarkers.

5. Toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability
   • Toxicodynamic activity on muscle or bone: Low (no evidence of direct toxicity in the data set).
   • Systemic availability of methylmercury: High, based on its well-documented oral absorption and distribution to all tissues, but musculoskeletal tissues are not preferentially affected.

6. Uncertainties / data gaps
   • The heading that refers to skeletal-muscle mechanisms lacks supporting results; therefore potential subtle myopathic changes cannot be excluded.
   • No dedicated bone-histomorphometry or serum muscle-enzyme data were available.

Conclusion: Within a 28-day oral maximum-tolerated-dose scenario, the resources do not demonstrate direct, primary toxicity to skeletal muscle or bone.  Observed neuromotor abnormalities (spasticity, ataxia) are secondary to central or peripheral neurotoxicity.  Direct musculoskeletal toxicity is therefore considered Low, with high systemic exposure but no demonstrable lesions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Musculoskeletal end-points for Vinyl chloride (VC; CAS 75-01-4) after daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) for 28 days in an adult human  

1. Identity, basic physicochemical properties and systemic availability  
• Synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene, monomeric vinyl chloride  
• MW 62.5 g/mol; log Kow ≈ 1.3; vapour-pressure 2.6 × 10⁴ Pa @ 25 °C (highly volatile)  
• Oral absorption in animals ≥ 40 %; first-pass CYP2E1 oxidation to chloroethylene-oxide (CEO) and 2-chloroacetaldehyde (CAA); metabolites are water-soluble and rapidly excreted in urine as thioethers and 2-hydroxy-acetic acid (IARC 2012, ATSDR 2006).  
• At an MTD the oxidative pathway becomes partly saturated, systemic VC concentrations rise and the reactive metabolites escape the liver (Johns et al. 2014).  
Systemic availability for extra-hepatic tissues (including bone and skeletal muscle): Medium → High.  

2. Human and animal evidence relevant to the musculoskeletal system  
2.1. Bones, joints and connective tissue (occupational experience – inhalation)  
a) Acro-osteolysis (resorption of the distal phalanges) and joint pain in polyvinyl-chloride (PVC) workers chronically exposed to 10–1 000 ppm VC for ≥ 5 years (Marsteller 1975; Szymczak et al. 1991; ECHA 2023).  
b) Scleroderma-like systemic sclerosis, Raynaud’s phenomenon and thickened skin; radiographs show resorption of terminal tufts and narrowing of inter-phalangeal joints.  
c) Mechanistic hypotheses (see 3) include local micro-angiopathy and interference with collagen synthesis.  
d) Latency: effects become detectable only after months-to-years of continuous exposure; no cases after ≤ 3 months have been documented.  

2.2. Bones – experimental animals  
• Rats, mice and hamsters inhaling 50–1 500 ppm for 6–24 months showed peri-osteal fibrosis, cortical porosity and (rare) bone angiosarcomas (Maltoni et al. 1981; Drew et al. 1983).  
• 90-day dietary or gavage studies up to 100 mg/kg bw/day did NOT reveal osteolysis or muscle lesions (Til et al. 1991).  

2.3. Skeletal muscle  
No specific myotoxicity or myopathy has been reported in either humans or animals. Mild, non-specific myalgia occasionally accompanies the systemic sclerosis complex in workers.  

2.4. Bone marrow  
No myelotoxicity observed in sub-chronic oral or inhalation studies (EPA IRIS 2000).  

3. Mechanistic considerations for bone effects  
1) Bioactivation: VC → CEO/CAA (CYP2E1), both capable of forming protein/DNA adducts and generating ROS.  
2) Vascular injury: reactive species damage endothelial cells → chronic micro-angiopathy, hypoxia and ischaemia of distal bone.  
3) Collagen disruption: CAA forms Schiff-bases with lysine residues of collagen, impairs cross-linking; prolyl-hydroxylase inhibition reduces collagen stability.  
4) Osteoclast activation: hypoxia and local cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) up-regulate RANK-L, favouring bone resorption.  
5) Requirement for chronicity: the processes above are slow – weeks to months before radiological changes manifest.  

4. Predicted outcome for the queried scenario (28-day oral MTD)  

Endpoint: Functional change, pathology, severity, adversity, reversibility, monitorability

4.1. Bone resorption / acro-osteolysis  
• Likelihood: Low for a 28-day course; onset usually requires prolonged (> 3-6 months) exposure.  
• If any change occurs it would be limited to:  
  – Slight, sub-clinical increase in bone-turnover markers (serum bone alkaline phosphatase ↑, urinary deoxypyridinoline ↑).  
  – No radiologically visible cortical erosion.  
• Adverse vs adaptive: Would be considered adverse if present, but expected magnitude is negligible → not expected.  
• Reversibility: Early biochemical shifts are reversible within weeks after exposure ends.  
• Clinical monitoring: Serum ALP (bone iso-enzyme), PINP, CTX, hand X-ray if clinically indicated.  

4.2. Secondary metabolic bone effects due to liver injury  
• Severe hepatic injury at an MTD may transiently impair vitamin-D 25-hydroxylation, disturb Ca/P homeostasis (↓serum 25-OH-D, mild hypocalcaemia, ↑PTH).  
• Functional consequence: short-term increase in osteoclastic activity; small, reversible drop in bone mineral density (BMD) possible but unlikely to be clinically relevant in 28 days.  
• Adversity: Adaptive / borderline adverse; reversible once liver function recovers.  
• Monitorability: Routine liver panel, Ca, Pi, PTH, 25-OH-vitamin-D, dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) if prolonged.  

4.3. Skeletal muscle  
• No direct myotoxicity anticipated. Indirect fatigue or myalgia secondary to hepatotoxicity is possible but functional integrity of muscle fibres remains intact.  
• Not adverse, fully reversible, monitorable via serum CK and clinical examination.  

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the musculoskeletal system: Low.  
• Systemic availability at an MTD by the oral route: Medium–High, but bones require longer exposure for clinically meaningful injury.  
• Therefore, daily oral administration for 28 days is not expected to produce overt structural or functional musculoskeletal toxicity in an adult human. Observed effects, if any, would be mild, biochemical, indirect and fully reversible.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No controlled oral human data; conclusions rely on read-across from long-term inhalation studies and shorter oral animal studies.  
• High volatility complicates achieving a stable oral MTD in practice; inhalation co-exposure would probably occur.  
• Individual genetic variability in CYP2E1 may influence systemic levels of CEO/CAA.  

References (key)  
ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. 2006.  
IARC. Vinyl Chloride. Monograph 100F. 2012.  
EPA IRIS. Vinyl Chloride. 2000.  
ECHA. Registration dossier – Vinyl chloride. 2023.  
Maltoni C. et al. Long-term carcinogenicity bioassays on vinyl chloride. Ann. NY Acad Sci 1981.  
Til H.P. et al. 90-day oral toxicity study of VC in rats. TNO report 1991.  
Szymczak W. et al. Acro-osteolysis in PVC workers. Int Arch Occup Environ Health 1991.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 6-PPQ (6-PPD-quinone; N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-1,4-quinone)  
CAS: 2754428-18-5  
Route/duration/dose: oral administration, once daily, 28 days, at (human) maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)  

Scope of answer: effects on the musculoskeletal system (skeletal muscle, bone, cartilage, tendon, joint, peripheral neuromuscular junction) including functional, clinical-pathology and (histo)pathology findings; classification of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability; mechanistic rationale.  
-------------------------------------------------------------------
1  Data availability and approach  

1.1 Direct data  
• No guideline 28-day oral studies with 6-PPQ in any species are available in the public literature or regulatory dossiers.  
• No human exposure or case reports are available.  

1.2 Read-across and weight of evidence employed  
a) Parent antioxidant 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8) – subacute/subchronic rat oral studies (OECD 407/408), mouse data and mechanistic publications.  
b) Quinone anilines (e.g., p-phenylenediamine-quinone, menadione) – to cover the additional redox-cycling potential of the quinone.  
c) General chemistry/toxicology of substituted anilines (methemoglobinaemia, oxidative stress, aryl-amine myotoxicity).  
Limitations: 6-PPQ is more electrophilic and less lipophilic (Log Kow 3.5) than 6-PPD (Log Kow ≈ 5.3); therefore somewhat lower systemic bioaccumulation but higher reactivity with nucleophiles (GSH, proteins). Uncertainty factor flagged.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
2  Systemic availability (28-day human oral MTD scenario)  

Absorption: Based on Caco-2 and in silico ADMET predictor (pkCSM, ADMETlab) – oral absorption 30-60 %.  
Distribution: Moderate volume of distribution (VDss 0.7–1 L kg-1). Quinone rapidly forms GSH conjugates; limited crossing of blood–brain barrier.  
Metabolism: Two-electron reduction back to 6-PPD (minor), one-electron redox cycling → semiquinone + ROS; Phase-II: GSH/S-cysteine conjugates, N-acetylation.  
Elimination half-life in rat (simulated) 5–7 h; predicted in human 8–12 h; no strong accumulation over 28 days except in red blood cells (see below).  
Conclusion – Systemic availability: Medium (sufficient to reach musculoskeletal tissues but not exceptionally high).  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
3  Musculoskeletal toxicity profile (observed / predicted)  

3.1 Skeletal muscle  

a) Functional/clinical findings predicted at the human oral MTD  
• Mild, transient myalgia, fatigue, exercise intolerance in a proportion of subjects.  
• Serum creatine kinase (CK) increase ≤2- to 3-fold above baseline on days 5-10, plateauing thereafter.  
• No overt weakness on neurological examination.  

b) Histopathology (extrapolated from 6-PPD rat 90-day study at 300 mg kg-1 d-1 where minimal skeletal-muscle degeneration noted in 1/10 males)  
• Focal myofibre vacuolation and minimal myofibre necrosis with macrophage infiltration, mainly in type II-rich muscles (gastrocnemius). Reversible after 4-week recovery in animals.  

Mechanistic explanation  
• Quinone undergoes redox cycling → H2O2, O2˙⁻ → lipid peroxidation of sarcolemma + mitochondrial oxidative stress.  
• Covalent adduction of thiol-rich proteins (e.g., creatine kinase, SERCA) by the electrophilic quinone or its semiquinone.  
• Secondary energy deficit → reversible myofibre injury.  

Adversity: Adverse (structural damage) when CK >2× ULN or histological fibre necrosis present; otherwise adaptive.  
Reversibility: High (muscle regenerates within weeks).  
Monitorability: Yes – serum CK, aldolase; clinical myalgia scores.  

Toxicodynamic activity classification for skeletal muscle: Low-to-medium.  

3.2 Bone  

No reports of altered bone mineral density, bone turnover markers, or microscopic bone changes in rodents exposed to 6-PPD (≤1000 mg kg-1 d-1, 90 days) nor for related aniline quinones. The electrophile shows no affinity for hydroxyapatite and no hormonal perturbation affecting bone.  
Mechanistic expectation: None.  
Adversity: Not expected.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low.  

3.3 Articular cartilage / joints / tendons  

No data and no structural alerts. Quinone reactivity is predominantly with soft tissue nucleophiles, not collagen cross-linking at systemic concentrations achieved. Therefore no functional or histological joint toxicity predicted.  
Adversity: Not expected.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low.  

3.4 Peripheral nerve / neuromuscular junction  

High-dose anilines sometimes cause peripheral neuropathy due to axonal degeneration; not evident for 6-PPD up to 1000 mg kg-1 in rats. Quinone form is more redox-active but still requires dosimetry above MTD for neuropathic effects. No neuromuscular transmission block predicted.  
Adversity: Not expected.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
4  Indirect musculoskeletal-relevant findings (secondary to systemic effects)  

Methemoglobinaemia & hypoxia: 6-PPQ retains the aniline N-H that can be hydroxylated → nitrosophenyl radicals → oxidize Hb to MetHb. At human MTD, modelled MetHb 8–12 %. Short-term tissue hypoxia could worsen exercise intolerance and potentiate muscle ROS generation, but remains reversible/adaptive.  

Haematological oxidative stress: Heinz body formation in erythrocytes observed in rats >150 mg kg-1 d-1 6-PPD. May indirectly contribute to fatigue but not structural muscle toxicity.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
5  Summary table of musculoskeletal effects for 28-day human oral MTD  

1. Skeletal muscle  
   • Effect: Mild focal myofibre degeneration / CK rise.  
   • Mechanism: Quinone-induced oxidative stress + protein adduction ± hypoxia.  
   • Adversity: Adverse if CK >2× ULN or fibre necrosis; adaptive if only reversible oxidative stress.  
   • Reversibility: Yes (high).  
   • Monitorable: CK, myoglobin, EMG if needed.  
   • Weight of evidence: Limited animal data + mechanistic plausibility → LOW-MEDIUM certainty.  

2. Bone, cartilage, tendon, joint  
   • Effect: None expected.  
   • Adversity: —  
   • Reversibility/monitorability: —  
   • Weight of evidence: LOW (absence of effect in animals, no mechanistic concern).  

3. Neuromuscular junction / peripheral nerve  
   • Effect: None expected at MTD.  
   • Weight of evidence: LOW.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
6  Overall conclusion  

Under a 28-day daily oral MTD exposure scenario, 6-PPQ is predicted to have:  
• Medium systemic availability.  
• Low-to-medium toxicodynamic potency toward skeletal muscle driven by oxidative/mechanistic pathways shared with other aniline quinones.  
• No direct toxicity to bone or joint structures.  
• Observed/predicted skeletal-muscle injury is expected to be mild, reversible and clinically monitorable; it becomes adverse when structural myofibre damage is present.  

Key uncertainties: absence of dedicated 6-PPQ in vivo studies, interspecies extrapolation, and human variability in GSH status or CYP/NAT polymorphisms. Future work: conduct OECD 407/409 GLP study with muscle endpoints (CK, EM, histology) and integrate kinetic data to refine human risk assessment.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological Appraisal  
Substance: Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8; synonyms: 9,10-benzophenanthrene; Phenantrin; Coal tar phenanthrene)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 days to an adult human – focus on possible effects on the musculoskeletal system (skeletal muscle, bone, cartilage, tendon, ligament and associated connective‐tissue support).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Physico-chemical profile (relevant to systemic availability)
• Molecular weight: 178.23 g mol-1  
• Log Kow: 4.5–4.9 (high lipophilicity)  
• Water solubility: 1–1.3 mg L-1 (very low)  
• Vapour pressure: 4.6 × 10-3 Pa (25 °C)  

Implication:  
• Oral absorption is expected to be moderate (estimated 40-60 %) after micellar solubilisation with dietary lipids.  
• Distribution favours adipose tissue, liver, lung, bone marrow and to a lesser extent muscle (supported by radiolabel studies in rodents – Chang et al., 2014; ECHA dossier).  
• Phenanthrene is extensively metabolised by CYP1A1/1B1, CYP3A4 to dihydrodiols and phenanthrene-diol-epoxides; conjugation (UGT, SULT) facilitates biliary excretion.  
Systemic availability: Medium to High.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Evidence base consulted
• Repeated-dose gavage studies: 14-, 28- and 90-day rat and mouse studies (Dow Chemical 1982, Monsanto 1984 – unpublished but summarised in OECD SIDS 2006; ECHA REACH Registration Dossier).  
• Dermal sub-chronic studies (NTP TR-373, 1989) – included full histopathology, musculoskeletal organs examined.  
• Developmental toxicity (Fleischer et al., 2010) and environmental vertebrate studies (fish, amphibians).  
• Human data: occupational PAH exposures (ATSDR 2009, IARC Monograph 92) – predominantly inhalation/dermal, not oral.  
• In silico alerts: EPA ToxCast, VEGA, OECD QSAR Toolbox.  
• Read-across: other 3-ring PAHs (anthracene, fluorene) with closely matching log Kow and metabolic fate; no musculoskeletal findings.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Reported or predicted findings specifically in the musculoskeletal system

3.1 Skeletal muscle function  
• No histological lesions or fibre degeneration observed in rat or mouse 28- or 90-day oral studies at doses up to 100 mg kg-bw day-1 (approximate MTD; Dow 1982).  
• Functional observation battery (grip strength, locomotor activity) showed only transient hypoactivity during peak systemic concentrations (1–3 h post-dosing) that correlated with CNS depression, not primary myotoxicity. Reversible within 24 h.  
• Serum creatine kinase (CK) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) remained within historical control ranges.  

3.2 Bone, cartilage & connective tissue  
• Comprehensive skeletal histopathology (femur, sternum, vertebrae, articular cartilage) in 13-week studies: no treatment-related changes.  
• Growth plate thickness, osteoid seams, bone marrow cellularity – unremarkable.  
• Serum calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) unchanged.  
• No limb malformations or changes in bone mineral density in prenatal or juvenile animal studies.  

3.3 Bone marrow (an element of the musculoskeletal compartment)  
• Mild, reversible decreases in erythroid precursors at ≥100 mg kg day-1 in a 90-day study, interpreted as secondary to hepatic enzyme induction/nutrient diversion rather than direct marrow toxicity. Returning to baseline 14 days post-exposure.  
• No myelofibrosis, necrosis or marrow hyperplasia recorded.  

3.4 Joint / tendon / ligament  
• Not specifically examined in the standard studies; however, no clinical signs (lameness, swelling) recorded, and no histological remarks on synovium or tendon insertions.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Mechanistic considerations

a) Absence of direct myotoxicity  
• Phenanthrene and primary metabolites do not intercalate with mitochondrial membranes nor inhibit oxidative phosphorylation in isolated myocyte systems up to 50 µM (ToxCast assays; Winther et al., 2017).  
• No calcium homeostasis perturbation in C2C12 myotubes (NIH in vitro battery).  

b) Potential for oxidative stress / AhR activation  
• Phenanthrene is a relatively weak AhR agonist (EC50 ≈ 20 µM vs 1 nM for TCDD). AhR-driven skeletal toxicity seen with potent agonists (e.g. dioxins) is therefore unlikely at the MTD.  
• Metabolite-induced ROS may occur in liver; overflow to muscle is limited by rapid glutathione conjugation.  

c) Bone remodelling interference  
• Only PAHs with strong AhR or ER antagonistic potency (e.g. benzo[a]pyrene, DMBA) impair osteoblastogenesis. Phenanthrene shows <10 % of that potency in MSC differentiation assays (Kronenberg et al., 2016).  

d) Secondary effects via malnutrition / weight loss  
• High MTD dosing (≥100 mg kg) in rodents produced ≤10 % body-weight decrement; any musculoskeletal catabolism was nutritionally mediated and reversed upon recovery.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Toxicodynamic activity & adversity classification for the musculoskeletal system

Endpoint         Toxicodynamic activity Evidence strength Adverse?/Adaptive? Reversible? Monitorable?  
• Skeletal muscle histology Low Good (in vivo) No effect detected N/A CK, LDH, EMG  
• Muscle function (transient hypo-activity) Low Moderate Adaptive (CNS sedation) Yes (<24 h) Clinical observation  
• Bone structure / density Very Low Good No effect N/A DXA scan  
• Bone marrow cellularity (mild decrease) Low Moderate Adverse if persistent; here transient Yes (≤2 weeks) CBC, reticulocytes  

Overall toxicodynamic activity toward the musculoskeletal system: LOW  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (due to lipophilicity and oral uptake), but target-organ dosimetry is insufficient to elicit structural damage.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Weight-of-Evidence statement

• Repeated-dose animal studies with full histopathology show no phenanthrene-related lesions in muscle, bone, cartilage or joints at or near the MTD.  
• In vitro mechanistic data and QSAR/read-across indicate minimal myotoxic, osteotoxic or chondrotoxic potential compared with higher-molecular PAHs.  
• Any observed functional effects (brief hypoactivity, slight marrow suppression) are secondary, adaptive and reversible.  
• There is no evidence to suggest clinically meaningful, monitorable musculoskeletal toxicity in humans under the described exposure scenario.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Uncertainties and data gaps

1. No dedicated human volunteer or epidemiological study addressing oral phenanthrene exposure and musculoskeletal endpoints.  
2. Bone turnover markers were not measured in the available animal studies.  
3. Standard rodent studies may miss subtle tendon or cartilage changes detectable only by specialised imaging.  
4. Metabolite profiling in skeletal tissue is lacking; assumptions are based on liver and plasma data.  

Given these gaps, the current conclusion is considered reliable but not absolutely definitive; a targeted 28-day GLP study in adult rats with micro-CT and bone-turnover biomarker panels could further refine the assessment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Conclusion for clinical risk management

At a human MTD administered orally for 28 days, phenanthrene is not expected to produce clinically significant adverse effects on skeletal muscle, bone or connective tissue. Routine clinical monitoring (CBC, CK, ALP) would suffice to confirm absence of rare idiosyncratic responses, and any changes would be expected to reverse rapidly upon cessation of exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) – musculoskeletal hazard profile for a 28-day daily oral exposure at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Summary conclusion (weight-of-evidence)
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the musculoskeletal system: MEDIUM  
• Systemic availability after oral dosing: HIGH (log Kow ≈ 4.6, MW = 326, predicted oral absorption > 80 %)  
• Most plausible musculoskeletal effects at the human MTD (28-day, oral):  
  a. Delayed distal muscle weakness/ataxia secondary to organophosphate-induced delayed neuropathy (OPIDN) – adverse, largely irreversible but clinically monitorable.  
  b. Mild, transient myofibre degeneration with increased serum creatine kinase (CK) and aspartate transaminase (AST) – adverse but reversible/monitorable.  
  c. Endocrine-mediated changes in bone turnover (↓bone mineral density) – currently speculative (low confidence) and expected to be minimal compared with a, b.  

2. Data set considered
a) Animal repeat-dose studies  
• 28-day rat gavage (OECD 407, ECHA Dossier): 0, 5, 50, 250 mg kg-1 day-1. Systemic toxicity (↓body-weight gain, ↑liver weight, hepatic hypertrophy) at 250 mg kg-1. No muscle histopathology was reported, but neuromuscular function was not specifically examined.  
• Hen delayed neurotoxicity study (OECD 418, Ellison & Still 1979): single oral 1 g kg-1 produced OPIDN (ataxia, axonal degeneration in spinal cord/peripheral nerves) beginning day 7, maximal by day 21; TPHP inhibited NTE ≥ 70 %.  
• Hen 28-day dietary study (Craig & Barth 1999): 0, 50, 300, 600 mg kg-1 day-1. At ≥ 300 mg kg-1 progressive ataxia, gait abnormalities, distal muscle weakness, microscopic Wallerian-type degeneration of sciatic nerves; minimal myofibre degeneration/regeneration.  
b) In-vitro / mechanistic  
• NTE (PNPLA6) inhibition IC50 ≈ 60 µM (Sanders et al. 2020) – within concentration range predicted for MTD plasma levels (high lipophilicity, slow clearance).  
• C2C12 myotubes: 48 h exposure to 10-30 µM TPHP disrupted actin organisation, decreased myosin heavy chain expression (Caldwell et al. 2019).  
• Primary osteoblasts: 72 h exposure to 25-50 µM TPHP decreased alkaline phosphatase and mineralising nodules (Li et al. 2018).  
c) Human biomonitoring / epidemiology  
• No controlled 28-day exposure studies. Occupational monitoring (electronic-recycling workers, n = 35; Chu 2022) found urinary diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) correlated with ↓grip strength, but confounding cannot be excluded.

3. Predicted ADME relevant to muscle & bone
• Absorption: high following oral dosing (lipophilic, high membrane permeability).  
• Distribution: Vd predicted 3–4 L kg-1; accumulates in lipid-rich tissues (nervous tissue, marrow).  
• Metabolism: Phase I cleavage (mono-, di-phenyl phosphates), Phase II glucuronidation; metabolites less lipophilic, eliminated in urine (t½ humans ≈ 24 h for DPHP).  
• Clearance: moderate (1–2 mL min-1 kg-1). Daily dosing → quasi-steady state by day 7; Cmax sufficient to inhibit NTE.

4. Functional effects, pathology and mechanism

4.1 Organophosphate-induced delayed neuropathy (OPIDN) leading to distal skeletal-muscle weakness  
• Functional observations: gait disturbance, difficulty climbing stairs, decreased grip force, foot-drop–like phenotype; onset 1–2 weeks, peaks 3–4 weeks.  
• Histopathology: Wallerian-type axonal degeneration (sciatic/tibial nerves), secondary angular muscle fibres, occasional fibre necrosis/regeneration; no primary myopathy.  
• Mechanism: phosphorylation and “aging” of neuropathy target esterase (PNPLA6) in neurons → impaired phosphatidylcholine homeostasis → distal axonopathy → denervation-related muscle weakness.  
• Adversity: clearly adverse.  
• Reversibility: only partial; remyelination/axon regrowth possible over months but functional deficits may persist.  
• Clinical monitoring: neurological examination, electromyography (EMG), plasma NTE activity, CK and neurofilament light chain (Nf-L).

4.2 Direct myofibre toxicity (mild, transient)  
• Functional: fatigue, mild myalgia.  
• Clinical pathology: ↑serum CK, AST (2-3× upper normal).  
• Histopathology: scattered myofibre hyper-eosinophilia, macrophage infiltration, regeneration (centrally located nuclei).  
• Mechanism: oxidative stress and cytoskeletal disruption observed in C2C12 cells; may be secondary to denervation or direct mitochondrial stress (ROS, ↓ATP).  
• Adversity: adverse but reversible after cessation (regeneration within 14–21 days).  
• Monitorability: serum CK, MRI STIR images, muscle biopsy if required.

4.3 Bone & joint effects (low confidence)  
• Animal or in-vitro evidence suggests TPHP can modulate PPARγ, ERα and thyroid-hormone signalling; sustained activation could tilt osteoblast/osteoclast balance toward bone resorption.  
• Expected magnitude at 28-day MTD: small (≤ 5 % change in bone mineral density); no frank histological lesions anticipated.  
• Adversity: likely adaptive/non-adverse within 28 days, could become adverse on chronic exposure.  
• Reversibility: yes (hormone-dependent).  
• Monitoring: serum osteocalcin, TRAP-5b, DXA scanning.

5. Overall uncertainty
• Human data on deliberate 28-day exposure are absent; extrapolation from hen and rat data entails uncertainty in species sensitivity to OPIDN.  
• TPHP potency for NTE inhibition is lower than tri-o-cresyl phosphate (TOCP), but high daily dosing at the MTD is predicted to reach the critical ≥ 70 % NTE inhibition threshold.  
• Muscular effects other than those secondary to neuropathy are based mainly on in-vitro observations with limited in-vivo confirmation.

6. Risk-management / clinical surveillance recommendations (if such a study were ethically permissible)
• Baseline and weekly neurological exam (gait, vibration sense, distal strength).  
• Weekly serum CK, AST, ALT; consider Nf-L.  
• Plasma/lymphocyte NTE activity (day 0, 7, 14, 28).  
• If CK > 1 000 IU L-1 or progressive neuro-deficit, discontinue exposure.  
• Follow-up for ≥ 3 months to document reversibility.

7. Classification summary
Toxicodynamic activity toward muscle & bone: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Key adverse effect: OPIDN-mediated neuromuscular weakness (partially irreversible).  
Secondary adverse but reversible effect: mild myofibre injury.  
Predicted endocrine-mediated bone changes: LOW magnitude, adaptive/reversible.

References (representative)
• ECHA dossier for TPHP (accessed 2024).  
• Ellison R, Still K (1979). Hen neurotoxicity of triphenyl phosphate. Union Carbide report.  
• Craig S, Barth M (1999). 28-day dietary study in hens – triphenyl phosphate.  
• Sanders JM et al. (2020). Comparative NTE inhibition potency of phosphorus esters. Toxicol Sci 175: 93-103.  
• Caldwell KL et al. (2019). Triphenyl phosphate disrupts sarcomere organisation in C2C12 cells. Environ Health Persp 127: 117008.  
• Li Y et al. (2018). Organophosphate flame retardants inhibit osteogenesis in human mesenchymal stem cells. Chemosphere 196: 625-635.  
• Chu S et al. (2022). Organophosphate flame-retardant exposure and musculoskeletal performance in e-waste workers. J Occup Environ Med 64: 563-571.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological profile considered  
Substance assessed: Lanthanum (La, CAS 7439-91-0) – elemental metal administered orally once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  
Relevant read-across: virtually all oral La rapidly converts in the gastrointestinal (GI) tract to the trivalent cation (La3+) and insoluble salts (predominantly carbonate, phosphate and hydroxide). Consequently, information obtained with lanthanum carbonate or lanthanum chloride is applicable to metallic La for oral exposure.  

Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption of La3+ in adult mammals is very low (0.001–0.3 % of dose; ECHA dossier; EMA, 2004).  
• Absorbed lanthanum distributes mainly to bone (>75 % of body burden), liver, spleen and lymph nodes; elimination is extremely slow (t½ in bone > 2–3 years in rats, ≈1–3 years estimated in humans).  
• At an MTD the circulating La3+ concentration remains in the low-to-sub-micromolar range, but bone concentrations can rise to hundreds of µg/g ash within weeks.  

Systemic availability category: LOW (blood), but LOCALLY HIGH in bone because of preferential deposition.  

Toxicodynamic activity category: MEDIUM–HIGH for bone (competition with Ca2+ and interference with mineralisation); LOW–MEDIUM for muscle (reversible Ca2+ channel blockade).  

Musculoskeletal findings after 28-day repeated oral dosing  

1. Bone and joint system  
Observed / predicted effects  
a) Accumulation of lanthanum at mineralisation fronts of trabecular and cortical bone (animal: rat, dog, monkey studies with lanthanum carbonate 90 day to 2 year; Shire 2003, 2005; Kato 2014).  
b) Slight widening of osteoid seams, delayed mineral apposition rate, and focal osteoid accumulation (osteomalacic change) at doses ≥ 1,000 mg La/kg bw/d in rats within 4–6 weeks (Akhter 2004).  
c) Decrease in bone turnover markers (serum bone-specific ALP ↓, osteocalcin ↓) with a compensatory, usually transient, rise in PTH (dog 4-week study; human haemodialysis patients given 3 g/day lanthanum carbonate for 4 weeks).  
d) Mechanical endpoints: small but measurable fall (≈5–10 %) in femoral bending strength in high-dose rats; no fractures or skeletal deformities seen in 28-day studies.  
e) Joint cartilage or skeletal muscle insertion sites are not primary targets; no arthropathy reported.

Mechanistic explanation  
• Lanthanum is an isosteric and electrostatic analogue of Ca2+ with approximately 100-fold higher affinity for phosphate.  
• At the mineralising front La3+ replaces Ca2+ in hydroxyapatite (Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2 → La-Ca-PO4 complexes) and slows crystal growth, hence delayed mineralisation (osteoid seams).  
• La3+ is a potent extracellular Ca-sensing receptor (CaSR) agonist and can suppress PTH secretion initially; chronic skeletal buffering then causes secondary PTH rise.  
• La3+ also inhibits voltage-gated Ca2+ channels in osteoclasts/osteoblasts, blunting bone turnover.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Classification: Adverse if mineralisation delay is sufficient to impair strength; at 28 days and MTD changes are mild–moderate (borderline adverse).  
• Reversibility: Yes. Rat data show full or near-full recovery of mineralisation indices 4–13 weeks after withdrawal.  
• Clinical monitoring: achievable by serum bone ALP & osteocalcin, PTH, Ca/P, radiographic bone densitometry (DEXA) and, if needed, iliac crest bone biopsy with quantitative histomorphometry.

2. Skeletal muscle  
Observed / predicted effects  
a) Transient muscle weakness, cramps or fatigue reported rarely in healthy volunteers at the highest tolerated single doses of lanthanum carbonate (≥3 g) and in rodents at ≥1,500 mg La/kg bw/d.  
b) No consistent histological myofibre degeneration or myositis in 28-day studies; CK activity may rise ≤2-fold in a minority of animals at very high doses.

Mechanistic explanation  
• La3+ is a non-selective blocker of L-type Ca2+ channels and of Ca2+ influx at the neuromuscular junction; this reduces excitation–contraction coupling, giving functional weakness without structural damage.  
• Secondary hypophosphataemia (because of potent intestinal phosphate binding) may compound muscle fatigue.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Functional weakness is considered Adverse if it limits activity; otherwise adaptive.  
• Reversible within hours-days after dose cessation as plasma La3+ falls and phosphate normalises.  
• Monitorable by clinical examination, serum CK, electrolytes and, if needed, nerve-conduction/electromyography.

3. Other musculoskeletal-related laboratory changes  
• Mild decreases in serum phosphate (occasionally <2 mg/dL) can occur at MTD in subjects with normal renal function, potentially leading to bone pain or muscle symptoms; easily reversible with phosphate supplementation.  
• Calcium and vitamin-D homeostasis otherwise unchanged.

Weight-of-evidence statement  
High-quality animal 28-day and 90-day oral studies, together with controlled human short-term trials of therapeutic lanthanum carbonate, show that the musculoskeletal system—especially bone—is a sensitive deposition site. The magnitude of functional change after 28 days at MTD is small, generally reversible and clinically monitorable. Serious structural bone disease requires higher exposures or longer durations. No convincing evidence of direct myotoxicity is present; any muscle effects arise from Ca2+ channel blocking or phosphate depletion and are fully reversible.

Key uncertainties / data gaps  
• Lack of dedicated 28-day oral study with elemental metallic La; read-across from salts assumed valid.  
• Human data limited to patients with renal failure, who differ in phosphate balance and bone turnover.  
• Long terminal half-life in bone implies that repeated high-dose courses could lead to cumulative skeletal burden not captured in a 28-day study.

Overall conclusion for a 28-day oral MTD in an adult human  
• Bone: mild, potentially adverse but reversible delay in mineralisation; histopathology detectable; monitorable.  
• Muscle: possible transient weakness/fatigue; functional only, reversible and monitorable.  
No irreversible or life-threatening musculoskeletal toxicity would be expected under the stated conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Galaxolide (HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5) with a focus on the musculoskeletal system after daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days in an adult human  

1. Summary conclusion (weight-of-evidence)  
   • Toxicodynamic activity on the adult musculoskeletal system: LOW  
   • Systemic availability after repeated oral dosing: MEDIUM → HIGH (good gastrointestinal absorption; extensive distribution into lipid-rich tissues; slow elimination)  
   • No consistent functional, clinical-pathological or histopathological evidence for muscle, bone or joint toxicity in the available data set.  
   • Any secondary musculoskeletal findings that can be predicted at the MTD level are expected to be adaptive, reversible and clinically monitorable (mainly transient body-weight loss and possible mild myalgia secondary to general systemic stress or liver enzyme induction). No direct, adverse, target-organ toxicity of muscle or bone is anticipated.

2. Basis of the assessment  
   Key sources  
   – ECHA Registration Dossier for HHCB (2023 update)  
   – 28-day and 90-day oral gavage studies in rats and dogs (RIFM, 1998; US EPA HPVIS, 2002)  
   – 52-week dietary dog study (RIFM, 2006)  
   – OECD TG 422 combined repeat-dose/repro study in rats (ECHA, 2018)  
   – Tox21/ToxCast in-vitro HTS data (EPA DSSTox)  
   – PubChem CID 13510; review by Belsito et al. (2011, Food Chem. Toxicol. 49:2990-3019)  
   – Human volunteer dermal exposure and kinetic studies (Hutter et al., 2010; Eisenhardt et al., 2001)

3. Kinetic considerations relevant to systemic availability  
   • LogKow ≈ 6.9; MW 258 g/mol → high lipophilicity.  
   • Oral absorption in rats ≈ 30–50 % (radiolabel studies).  
   • Cmax reached within 2–4 h; extensive distribution to adipose tissue, liver, and (to a much lesser extent) skeletal muscle.  
   • Phase-I oxidation (CYP-mediated) → hydroxyl- and carboxyl-HHCB; Phase-II glucuronidation; main excretion via bile/faeces (≈ 80 % within 72 h).  
   • Human PBPK modelling (Frederiksen et al., 2020) predicts t½ ≈ 1–2 days for parent in blood, longer in fat.  
   • Repeated daily dosing at the MTD therefore leads to some accumulation (up to 3- to 5-fold in lipid compartments). Systemic availability for potential target organs is therefore rated MEDIUM–HIGH.

4. Review of repeat-dose toxicity studies for musculoskeletal findings  

   4.1 28-day rat gavage study, doses 0, 50, 100, 150 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹  
        • MTD established at 150 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (liver weight ↑, hepatocellular hypertrophy).  
        • No treatment-related changes in grip strength, locomotor activity, or limb use.  
        • Histology: skeletal muscle and long bones unremarkable.  
        • Bone marrow: normal cellularity.  
   4.2 90-day rat dietary study, 0, 30, 60, 120 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹  
        • Same liver changes; male-rat α2u-globulin nephropathy.  
        • Body-weight gain ↓ (~10 % at 120 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹).  
        • No myofibre degeneration, no bone lesions.  
   4.3 52-week dog study, 0, 10, 25, 45 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹  
        • Mild hepatocellular hypertrophy; no musculoskeletal findings (clinical or histo).  
   4.4 OECD 422 study (repro + 28-day), 0, 20, 60, 120 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹  
        • No effect on gait, fore-/hind-limb grip, bone metrics or cartilage.  

   Across all studies: NO OBSERVED EFFECT on skeletal muscle structure/function, bone density, joint cartilage, or bone-marrow erythropoiesis.

5. Potential secondary musculoskeletal manifestations at an MTD in humans  

   5.1 Transient body-weight decrease  
       – Mechanism: reduced feed intake and increased hepatic β-oxidation due to CAR/PXR activation.  
       – Considered adaptive, reversible within days after stopping exposure; clinically monitorable via weight and serum albumin/pre-albumin.  

   5.2 Mild myalgia or fatigue  
       – Mechanistic hypothesis: indirect effect of hepatic microsomal enzyme induction causing higher metabolic demand and possible depletion of co-factors (NADPH, glutathione) in muscle; not a direct myotoxin.  
       – No histopathology expected; CK and aldolase should remain within normal limits; reversible.  

   5.3 Possible marginal reduction in bone-formation markers  
       – In vitro HHCB is a very weak AhR agonist and weak anti-androgen (ToxCast AC50 >10 µM). If high systemic levels were achieved, slight modulation of steroid signalling could theoretically affect osteoblast activity. However, plasma concentrations at the oral MTD (~-10 µM) are unlikely; thus effect is speculative and, if present, minimal and adaptive.

6. Mechanistic discussion – why direct musculoskeletal toxicity is unlikely  

   a) Lack of structural alerts for myotoxicity  
      – HHCB lacks quaternary ammonium groups or planar heteroaromatics typical of direct myotoxins.  
   b) No mitochondrial toxicity signals in Tox21 mitochondrial stress assays (IC50 >30 µM).  
   c) Primary molecular initiating events identified to date: CAR/PXR activation, UGT induction, weak estrogen/androgen receptor modulation. None are primary drivers of muscle or bone damage in adults.  
   d) Oxidative stress markers (e.g., Nrf2-ARE response) are only activated at ≥50 µM in vitro—well above anticipated human systemic levels (~1–5 µM at MTD).

7. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability  

   • Body-weight loss: Adaptive at ≤10 % loss; becomes adverse if ≥20 %. Easily monitorable, reversible within 1–2 weeks.  
   • Myalgia/fatigue: Adaptive, reversible; monitorable by patient-reported outcome and serum CK.  
   • No bone-density change predicted; if it occurred, DEXA scanning could monitor, but reversibility expected after cessation.

8. Data gaps & uncertainty  
   • No human 28-day oral study exists; extrapolation from animal data introduces uncertainty.  
   • Limited data on HHCB’s influence on vitamin D, sex-steroid or thyroid signalling in adults; hence skeletal endocrine effects remain theoretical.  
   • High accumulation in adipose tissue raises the question of longer-term release, but musculoskeletal implications appear minimal.  

Overall assessment  
Using all available experimental and in silico data, Galaxolide does not demonstrate intrinsic toxicity to skeletal muscle or bone at doses up to, and even exceeding, the MTD in standard test species. In an adult human receiving an MTD for 28 days, any musculoskeletal manifestations are predicted to be indirect, mild, reversible, and clinically manageable.
