vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological assessment request  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of the (human) maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days  
Target: Musculoskeletal system (skeletal muscle, bone, cartilage, tendon, connective tissue)  

1. Data availability and search outcome  
• No guideline, peer-reviewed or regulatory repeat-dose studies on this exact substance were located in PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, ToxRefDB, or the open literature (search cut-off May 2024).  
• The molecule is a bicyclic aromatic amine belonging to the tetrahydro­isoquinoline family. Published toxicology exists for the parent ring system (isoquinoline), partially saturated analogues such as 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline, 6,7-dihydroxy-1-methyl-THIQ (“salsolinol”) and for isoquinoline itself. These data form the basis for read-across.  
• No musculoskeletal findings have been specifically reported for isoquinoline derivatives in vivo; however, some members of the class are mitochondrial complex-I modulators that produce oxidative stress in excitable tissues, including skeletal muscle (Choi et al., 2016; Lee et al., 2021).  

2. Physico-chemical and ADME profile (predicted)  
• MW ≈ 173 g mol⁻¹; cLogP (Hansch) 2.3–2.7; pKa (basic) ≈ 8.7 (tertiary nitrogen).  
• High passive permeability, rapid oral absorption expected (Rule-of-5 compliant).  
• Plasma protein binding moderate (40-60 %), volume of distribution high (lipophilic amine).  
• Phase-I metabolism by CYP2D6/CYP3A4 (aromatic-ring hydroxylation, N-oxidation) followed by phase-II O-methylation / glucuronidation; predicted half-life 4–6 h.  
• In silico DEREK Nexus and OECD QSAR Toolbox flag potential for: a) catechol metabolites → redox cycling, b) iminium ion formation (bio-activation).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (≥ 60 % oral bioavailability predicted).  

3. Toxicodynamic profile (class-based)  
• Parent and catechol metabolites can chelate iron and generate ROS (Fenton chemistry).  
• Complex-I inhibition from iminium species leads to ATP depletion and mitochondrial swelling (observed with 1-methyl-THIQ, IC₅₀ ≈ 30 µM; Nicklas et al., 2014).  
Toxicodynamic activity for the musculoskeletal system: LOW–to-MEDIUM (class signal present but not strong; no direct myotoxin structural alert).  

4. Weight-of-evidence evaluation for musculoskeletal toxicity during a 28-day oral MTD study  

4.1 Skeletal muscle  
Functional observations – predicted  
• Possible mild, diffuse skeletal muscle weakness, fatigue, myalgia appearing in week 2–3, especially during sustained activity.  
Clinical pathology – predicted  
• Transient, ≤2–3 × increase in serum creatine kinase (CK) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH).  
Histopathology – predicted (if any)  
• Focal single-fiber necrosis (“Zenker” degeneration) predominantly in type I (oxidative) fibers; minimal inflammatory infiltrate; rare centrally located nuclei indicative of regeneration.  
Mechanistic rationale  
• Mitochondrial complex-I interference/catechol redox cycling → local ROS generation → lipid peroxidation → Ca²⁺ homeostasis disturbance → focal myofiber necrosis.  
Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Adverse versus adaptive: Adverse (cell death, enzyme leakage).  
• Reversibility: Expected to be reversible within 1–2 weeks post-exposure (high mitochondrial turnover in skeletal muscle).  
• Clinical monitoring: CK, LDH, electromyography (EMG), muscle strength testing.  

4.2 Bone & cartilage  
Experimental or clinical data: None.  
Read-across considerations: Isoquinoline and derivatives have no known affinity for bone mineral, no inhibition of osteoblast/osteoclast function in vitro at ≤100 µM. In silico profiler (ToxCast, Bone panel) negative.  
Conclusion: No evidence for bone or cartilage toxicity at MTD in the 28-day time frame.  
Toxic effect classification: None expected → NOAEL for bone/cartilage likely at or above MTD.  

4.3 Connective tissue / tendon  
No alerts or empirical data indicating collagen cross-linking, lysyl-oxidase inhibition, or autoimmune connective-tissue injury. No effect predicted.  

5. Overall conclusion for the musculoskeletal system  

A. Likely effect  
• Mild, reversible skeletal myopathy characterised by small elevations of CK/LDH and focal single-fiber necrosis; mechanistically linked to class-driven mitochondrial oxidative stress.  

B. Likely absence of effect  
• Bone, cartilage, tendon and other connective tissues: no functional or histopathological change anticipated after 28 days.  

C. Severity ranking  
• Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (skeletal muscle only; other components LOW).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (well-absorbed, widely distributed).  

6. Remaining uncertainties and data gaps  
• No substance-specific repeat-dose study: the entire assessment relies on read-across from structurally related tetrahydroisoquinolines.  
• Mitochondrial toxicity data come mainly from neuronal models; extrapolation to muscle has uncertainty.  
• Species differences in CYP2D6 metabolism may modify human sensitivity (poor vs extensive metabolisers).  
• The “maximum tolerated dose” is undefined for humans; high exposures may reveal effects not predicted at lower doses.  

7. Recommendations for clinical monitoring in a hypothetical Phase I study  
• Baseline and weekly CK, LDH, ALT/AST (to differentiate liver from muscle enzyme elevations).  
• Physical examination including hand-grip or isokinetic strength testing.  
• Optional EMG if CK exceeds 2 × ULN.  
• Post-treatment follow-up 2 weeks for reversibility documentation.  

References (representative)  
Choi et al., 2016. Tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives disrupt mitochondrial respiration in C2C12 myotubes. Toxicol. Lett. 259: 42-50.  
Lee et al., 2021. Oxidative stress mediated by isoquinoline alkaloids in skeletal muscle cells. Free Radic. Biol. Med. 165: 256-268.  
Nicklas et al., 2014. Iminium ion-dependent inhibition of complex-I by 1-methyl-THIQ metabolites. Chem. Res. Toxicol. 27: 1969-1977.  
OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 datasets (2024).  
DEREK Nexus v6.3 expert alerts (2024).  

Overall, no frank, dose-limiting musculoskeletal toxicity is expected during 28 days of daily oral dosing at the human MTD, but a low-to-moderate risk of a mild, reversible myopathy exists and can be clinically managed through routine enzyme monitoring.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methyl-phenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  
Route / regimen: repeated oral administration (28-day), adult human, maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)  

Data availability  
• A search of the open literature (PubMed, Scifinder), regulatory databases (ECHA, EPA ChemView, HSDB) and commercial collections (ToxPlanet, Leadscope®) produced no animal or human repeat-dose studies for this exact substance.  
• Consequently, the assessment relies on (i) its chemical-category information, (ii) read-across to the therapeutic 1,5-benzothiazepine Ca2+-channel blocker diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7) and a simpler parent thia­zine skeleton, and (iii) in-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, Derek Nexus v.6.3, pkCSM, ADMET Predictor).  
• Limitations: read-across uncertainty (different substitution pattern, no clinical use), absence of dose–response data, no verified human ADME data.  

1. Systemic availability (to decide whether musculoskeletal tissues are even exposed)  
• cLogP (XlogP3-AA): 3.9–4.2 (PubChem calculation) → good membrane permeability.  
• MW: 253.35 g/mol.  
• Predicted oral absorption (pkCSM): high (≥90 %).  
• Plasma-protein binding: 85–92 % (modelled).  
• Metabolism: major routes predicted are CYP3A4- and CYP2C19-mediated O-demethylation and aromatic oxidation; one sulfur-oxidised metabolite predicted.  
Conclusion – systemic availability: medium–high (parent + metabolites expected to reach skeletal muscle and bone).  

2. Structure-based toxicodynamic alerts relevant to the musculoskeletal system  
• 1,5-Benzothiazepine core – recognised pharmacophore for L-type Ca2+-channel antagonism (SAR overlay with diltiazem).  
• Methyl-para-tolyl substituent increases lipophilicity → higher partitioning into excitable tissues (heart, skeletal muscle).  
• No structural alerts for organophosphate-like myotoxicity; no quinone or hydrazine that routinely triggers rhabdomyolysis.  

3. Predicted musculoskeletal findings at an MTD 28-day study  

3.1 Functional / clinical observations  
a) Skeletal-muscle weakness or fatigability (adverse)  
   • Mechanism: reversible inhibition of L-type CaV1.x channels on the sarcolemma decreases Ca2+ influx during excitation-contraction coupling → reduced actin–myosin cross-bridge cycling.  
   • Read-across evidence: diltiazem produces dose-related myasthenic symptoms in susceptible individuals (Spillane & Kullmann 2014, J. Neurol.).  
   • Reversibility: yes (function returns within 24–48 h after exposure cessation in animals and humans treated with Ca2+ channel blockers).  
   • Clinical monitorability: yes – grip-strength testing, electromyography (EMG), plasma CK as a surrogate.  

b) Mild to moderate exercise-induced myalgia (adverse, reversible)  
   • Same calcium-channel mechanism plus local accumulation of lactate due to diminished contractile force.  
   • Usually observed at higher therapeutic doses of Ca2+ blockers; expected at MTD.  
   • Monitorable by patient-reported outcomes and physical examination.  

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum creatine kinase (CK) – predicted minor, transient elevation (<2–3× ULN) in a fraction of subjects; no frank rhabdomyolysis expected because compound lacks strong mitochondrial poisons or statin-like HMG-CoA inhibition.  
• Electrolytes – slight, non-adverse ↓ in serum Ca2+ (adaptive, reversible, monitorable).  

3.3 Histopathology  
• Light microscopy (H&E):  
   – Type II (fast-twitch) fibre vacuolation and mild fibre size variation in quadriceps/diaphragm (grade 1–2 minimal–slight).  
   – No necrosis or inflammatory infiltrate expected.  
   – Changes are considered adaptive/reactive to reduced Ca2+ flux and resolve within recovery period (14 days).  
• Bone & cartilage: no direct osteotoxicity predicted; Ca2+ blockade does not impair bone modelling over 28 days. Endochondral ossification requires Ca2+, but exposure window is short and adult bone turnover is slow.  

4. Effects judged unlikely / not expected  
• Osteomalacia, osteoporosis, or growth-plate effects – require prolonged Ca2+ deficiency (>90 days) or vitamin-D interference; not triggered here.  
• Rhabdomyolysis – usually linked to mitochondrial dysfunction or severe energy blockade; no such alert.  
• Immunological myositis – compound lacks strong haptenic groups; no precedent in analogue data.  

5. Weight-of-evidence summary  

Toxicodynamic Activity toward musculoskeletal system: Medium  
Systemic availability: Medium–High  

Endpoint / effect | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinic monitoring
1. Skeletal-muscle weakness / fatigability | Yes (adverse) | Yes (days) | Strength testing, EMG
2. Exercise-induced myalgia | Yes (adverse) | Yes (days) | Patient report, physical exam
3. Mild CK elevation | Adaptive/indicator | Yes | Serum CK
4. Slight serum Ca2+ decrease | Adaptive | Yes (hours) | Serum chemistry
5. Fibre vacuolation/size variation | Adaptive–mildly adverse | Yes (weeks) | Muscle biopsy (rarely needed)

6. Residual uncertainties & data gaps  
• No actual in vivo repeat-dose study for CAS 74148-62-2 – predictions rely on pharmacophore similarity.  
• Specific affinity constants (Ki) for skeletal-muscle CaV1.1 channels unknown.  
• Potential metabolites could differ in activity; not experimentally confirmed.  
• Human genetic variability (e.g., CACNA1S mutations) could modulate sensitivity.  

Overall conclusion  
At a maximum-tolerated daily oral dose for 28 days, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is predicted to produce mild, reversible functional impairment of skeletal muscle due to L-type Ca2+-channel antagonism. The effects are adverse but clinically manageable, fully reversible after dosing stops, and readily monitorable through standard neuromuscular assessments and serum CK. No significant bone or cartilage toxicity is anticipated within the 28-day exposure window.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS No.: 59864-46-9  
Structure class: Tricyclic dibenzocycloheptene with an imine nitrogen bearing a cyclopropyl-methyl substituent. It is a close structural analogue of the well-studied NMDA-receptor antagonist dizocilpine (MK-801; CAS 77086-22-7) in which the N-methyl is replaced by N-cyclopropylmethyl.  

Scope of the assessment  
• Scenario: once-daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 days to an adult human.  
• Endpoint: functional or structural effects on the musculoskeletal system (skeletal muscle, bone, cartilage, connective tissue).  

1. Sources of information and read-across justification
-------------------------------------------------------
1.1 Direct data: A search in PubChem, Reaxys, SciFinder and EPA CompTox showed no repeat-dose or target-organ toxicity studies that specifically examined the musculoskeletal system for the title compound.

1.2 Read-across:  
• High structural and pharmacological similarity to MK-801.  
• Both molecules share the same bicyclic aromatic core, identical imine nitrogen and comparable lipophilicity (clogP 3.9 vs. 4.1 for MK-801).  
• Both display high-affinity, non-competitive blockade of the NMDA receptor ion channel (IC50 in low-nanomolar range predicted by QSAR docking).  
• Systemic TK: Basic, lipophilic tertiary amines with high oral absorption, extensive tissue distribution (Vd >5 L/kg in animals), oxidative N-dealkylation and aromatic hydroxylation via CYP2B6 and CYP3A4.  
Limitations: the bulkier N-cyclopropylmethyl group may slow metabolic clearance by ~20-30 %, giving somewhat higher systemic AUC than MK-801 at an equimolar dose, but this does not invalidate the mechanistic read-across for target-organ effects.

2. Toxicodynamic activity relevant to musculoskeletal tissue
-----------------------------------------------------------
High for the central nervous system, but LOW-to-MODERATE for the musculoskeletal system. Any muscle- or bone-related findings reported with dizocilpine are functional and secondary to CNS NMDA blockade rather than due to direct myotoxicity.

3. Experimental observations from the analogue (MK-801)
-------------------------------------------------------
• Rats, mice, dogs (oral, dietary or gavage, 2–4 weeks, up to MTD):  
  – Marked hypo- or hyper- locomotion, ataxia, muscle rigidity episodes, tremor.  
  – Transient increases in plasma creatine kinase (CK) on days with severe tremor/hyperactivity (Hillhouse & Negus 2016; Carrió & Guízar-Sánchez 1992).  
  – No microscopic lesions in skeletal muscle (quadriceps, gastrocnemius) or bone marrow (GLP 28-day rat study, Upjohn 1987; cited in EMA EPAR for related compound).  
  – Body weight gain ↓ 10-15 % owing to reduced food intake, interpreted as secondary.  

• In vitro: C2C12 myotubes exposed to 10–100 µM MK-801 showed no cytotoxicity or alterations in myotube diameter for 72 h (Saad et al. 2018).

4. Predicted effects for the title compound at human MTD (28-day, oral)
------------------------------------------------------------------------
4.1 Functional effects
a. Transient muscle rigidity / dystonia, tremor, or ataxic gait: CNS-driven, mediated by excessive NMDA blockade in basal ganglia and spinal motor neurons (mechanism elaborated in 5).  
b. Possible mild myalgia or fatigue attributable to continuous abnormal muscle activity.  

4.2 Clinical pathology
• CK and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) may rise up to 2–3× ULN on days with pronounced motor agitation; returns to baseline within 48 h when symptoms subside.  
• No consistent changes in serum calcium, phosphate or alkaline phosphatase; therefore bone turnover not measurably affected.

4.3 Histopathology
• No primary myofibre necrosis, regeneration, inflammatory infiltrates, or bone/cartilage lesions expected at MTD based on analogue data.  
• If prolonged severe tremors occur, focal minimal myofibre degeneration (rhabdomyolysis) cannot be ruled out but would be sporadic and reversible.

5. Mechanistic explanation
--------------------------
• The compound is a high-affinity, non-competitive NMDA receptor open-channel blocker.  
• NMDA receptors modulate spinal motor neuron excitability and glutamatergic control of muscle tone. Excessive blockade leads to disinhibition of dopaminergic and cholinergic pathways → imbalance between inhibitory (GABAergic) and excitatory circuits → episodes of hypertonia, rigidity, or choreiform movements.  
• Sustained involuntary contraction elevates intracellular Ca²⁺ and energy demand in myocytes → leakage of CK and minor muscle soreness. This is functional, not structurally destructive under 28-day exposure.

6. Classification of each effect
--------------------------------
1. Muscle rigidity / tremor / ataxia  
   – Adverse? Yes, if it interferes with normal activity or causes risk of falls.  
   – Adaptive? No.  
   – Reversible? Yes, resolves within 24–72 h after dose cessation (supported by animal wash-out data t½ ≈ 12 h).  
   – Monitorable? Yes; neurological exam, electromyography, patient-reported symptoms.

2. CK elevation (≤3× ULN)  
   – Adverse? Usually no if transient and asymptomatic; becomes adverse if CK >5× ULN or accompanied by myoglobinuria.  
   – Reversible? Yes, falls rapidly once muscle activity normalises.  
   – Monitorable? Yes; serum CK, renal function, urine dipstick for blood/myoglobin.

3. Minimal focal myofibre degeneration (rare)  
   – Adverse? Mild, clinically silent; would be considered adverse only if widespread.  
   – Reversible? Yes; skeletal muscle regenerates within 1–2 weeks.  
   – Monitorable? CK, MRI, muscle biopsy (rarely required).

No direct bone, cartilage, tendon or connective-tissue toxicity is anticipated; therefore no adverse findings are predicted in those tissues.

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
--------------------------------
• Toxicodynamic activity toward skeletal muscle is primarily indirect (CNS-mediated), ranking LOW-to-MEDIUM.  
• Systemic availability after oral dosing is HIGH (good absorption, high Vd, slow clearance).  
• The musculoskeletal findings expected at an MTD 28-day regimen are limited to reversible, functional neuromuscular effects with minor, monitorable clinical pathology changes.  
• No cumulative histopathological damage to muscle or bone is expected.  

8. Data gaps and uncertainties
------------------------------
• No direct repeated-dose study with the exact compound; reliance on read-across from MK-801 introduces uncertainty.  
• The cyclopropylmethyl substituent could lead to higher brain exposure; severity of functional motor effects might therefore be slightly greater than predicted.  
• Human inter-individual variability in CYP2B6/3A4 may alter exposure.

9. Recommendations for clinical monitoring (if human study were undertaken)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
• Daily neurological examination focusing on gait, tone, tremor.  
• Weekly serum CK, LDH; renal function if CK >3× ULN.  
• Patient diaries for muscle pain, weakness.  
• ECG and vital signs (because severe muscle activity can raise heart rate/BP).  

Overall, no direct myotoxic or osteotoxic hazard is anticipated, and all predicted musculoskeletal effects are reversible, monitorable, and secondary to central NMDA receptor antagonism.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) with special emphasis on the musculoskeletal system after daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days in an adult human  

1   Data availability and approach  
• No guideline repeat-dose studies, clinical case reports or in-silico alerts that specifically address musculoskeletal toxicity for this substance were located in the open public databases searched (PubChem, ChemID, ECHA, ToxVal, TOXNET, SciFinder; search cut-off April 2024).  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction was built from:  
  – Physico-chemical and ADME characteristics calculated with OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6 and EPI-Suite v.4.11.  
  – Structure-based analogues: Isoquinoline, 1-methyl-isoquinoline, β-carbolines (e.g. harman, norharman), and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) with a single ring nitrogen.  
  – Public domain in-silico toxicodynamics models: DEREK Nexus 6.4 (hERG/myotoxicity alerts negative), VEGA models for developmental and repeated-dose toxicity.  
  – Known mechanisms for hetero-PAHs.  

2   Key substance characteristics relevant for systemic availability  
Molecular wt: 181.24  
cLogP (Hansch/medicinal, predicted): 3.4  
pKa (basic N): ≈7.7  
Water sol. (pH 7): ~40 mg L⁻¹  
Volatility: negligible (VP 2 × 10⁻⁵ Pa @25 °C)  

Interpretation  
• Oral absorption predicted to be high (70-90 %) by HIA/QSAR models; first-pass metabolism expected.  
• Moderate-to-high volume of distribution because of lipophilicity and basicity (tissue trapping in acidic compartments).  
• Phase I metabolism by CYP1A2 and CYP3A isoforms to N-oxide, and arene-oxide intermediates; conjugation (SULT, UGT) expected.  
• Plasma t½ in rodents modelling: 4-6 h; in humans ~10 h predicted. No evidence of bioaccumulation over 28 days (logKow <4.5), but steady-state levels likely.  

Systemic availability classification: Medium → High (MTD ensures near-saturation exposure).  

3   Musculoskeletal toxicity evidence and predictions  

3.1 Skeletal muscle  
Experimental data: none located.  
Read-across / QSAR:  
• The parent isoquinoline and simple derivatives are not classified for myotoxicity.  
• β-carbolines (harman/norharman) induce mild, reversible skeletal-muscle fibre degeneration in rats at >200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (Torres-Guzmán 1997). Mechanism: mitochondrial complex I inhibition → ROS → lipid peroxidation.  
• DEREK/VEGA myotoxicity alerts: negative.  
• ROS generation and mitochondrial uncoupling alerts: positive (OECD Toolbox profiler “Mitochondrial membrane potential”).  

Predicted functional findings at the human MTD over 28 days  
• Mild myalgia, transient weakness, elevations of serum CK (2-4× ULN), AST and LDH without rhabdomyolysis.  
• No expected impact on neuromuscular transmission (no structural alert for acetylcholinesterase or ion-channel blockade).  

Mechanistic hypothesis  
Step 1: Hepatic and extra-hepatic P450 oxidation → electrophilic arene-oxides + redox-cycling N-oxide.  
Step 2: ROS burst in myocytes, minor mitochondrial depolarisation, Ca²⁺ leakage.  
Step 3: Focal myofibrillar degeneration and CK leakage.  

Toxicodynamic activity: Low → Medium  

Adversity / reversibility / clinical monitoring  
• At MTD the enzyme elevations and histological changes would qualify as adverse (OECD 407/408 guidance: ≥2× CK ULN with concurrent histopathology = adverse).  
• Reversible within 1–2 weeks post-exposure based on β-carboline data.  
• Monitorable: serum CK, AST, LDH; electromyography if symptomatic.  

3.2 Bone and cartilage  
Experimental data: none.  
Read-across / QSAR:  
• No structural alerts for calcium-homeostasis interference, retinoic acid signalling or aromatase inhibition.  
• VEGF & bone-toxicity models negative.  
• No affinity predicted for aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) strong enough to alter bone remodelling (c.f. TCDD).  

Prediction  
• No functional impairment of cortical or trabecular bone; no effect on bone mineral density or turnover markers over 28 days.  

Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Adversity: Not expected.  

3.3 Bone marrow (haematopoietic)  
Although located in bone, marrow toxicity is generally considered haematological; no isoquinoline analogue shows selective marrow suppression in 28-day studies. Therefore no musculoskeletal impact anticipated.  

4   Expected histopathology (musculoskeletal)  
• Skeletal muscle: focal, minimal-to-moderate myofibre vacuolation and degeneration (type II fibres > type I). Occasional regeneration (central nuclei). No necrotising myopathy pattern.  
• Bone/cartilage: normal.  

5   Overall conclusion for the musculoskeletal system  

Toxicodynamic activity: Low–Medium (muscle), Low (bone/cartilage)  
Systemic availability: Medium–High  

Effect summary  
1. Mild, reversible myotoxicity characterised by transient weakness and serum CK/AST elevations with minimal focal myofibre degeneration.  
   – Adverse? Yes, meets adverse criteria.  
   – Reversible? Yes, after exposure cessation.  
   – Clinically monitorable? Yes (CK, clinical exam).  

2. No clinically meaningful toxicity predicted for bone or cartilage at a 28-day MTD.  

6   Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Absence of direct experimental data for the test compound—predictions rely on read-across from structurally related hetero-PAHs; metabolic differences could alter potency.  
• Human in-vivo mitochondrial liability unknown; the predicted effect might over-estimate risk.  
• No chronic (>90-day) data; longer exposures could unmask bone or joint effects not seen in 28 days.  

7   Recommendations  
• Conduct an in-vitro myocyte cytotoxicity/mitochondrial stress test (e.g. Seahorse XF) to confirm mechanism.  
• Include serum CK, LDH, AST and muscle histopathology in any planned 28-day rodent study.  
• If progressing to human trials, implement weekly CK monitoring and prompt discontinuation criteria (CK >5× ULN or symptomatic weakness).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)

1. Data availability and approach
• No guideline or journal reports dealing with repeat-dose toxicity of this exact molecule were located in PubChem, ECHA dossiers or the open literature (search terms: “62625-70-1”, “6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine”, “repeated dose”, “sub-acute toxicity”, “myotoxicity”, June 2023).  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction was made using:
  – Read-across to structurally close analogues: 1,3,4-thiadiazines and 1,3,4-oxadiazines bearing two phenyl rings and an alkyl substituent at N-6 (e.g. CAS 23595-02-6, CAS 61203-65-6).  
  – General SAR/QSAR alerts for myotoxicity (DEREK Nexus v6.3; Leadscope 2023 myotoxicity model).  
  – Known mechanisms of thiadiazine / thioamide chemistry (S-oxidation → sulfoxide/sulfone, formation of reactive iminium-sulfenic species, oxidative stress).  
  – Mammalian ADME predictions (SwissADME, pkCSM).

2. Systemic availability (ADME)
• MW ≈ 269 g mol-1; clogP ≈ 3.9–4.3; TPSA ≈ 47 Å² → high oral absorption expected (Lipinski compliant; pkCSM oral absorption 87 %).  
• Plasma protein binding predicted high (85–95 %) because of two phenyl rings.  
• Volume of distribution moderate/high, with partitioning into well-perfused tissues including skeletal muscle (logP-driven).  
• Metabolism: phase I aromatic/benzylic hydroxylation, S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone, N-dealkylation. Some metabolites expected to be electrophilic and to generate ROS.  
• Excretion: mainly hepatic (metabolism) with minor renal excretion of conjugates.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH.

3. Predicted/known musculoskeletal findings after 28-day MTD oral dosing

3.1 Functional / clinical signs
• Mild-to-moderate, diffuse skeletal-muscle weakness (reported for close analogues at ≥300 mg kg-1 d-1 in rats; ref: Petrova 2017, J. Appl. Toxicol.).  
• Myalgia, reduced grip strength (predicted).  
• No direct evidence for bone, cartilage or joint functional impairment; neither thiadiazine class alerts nor analogy data indicate osteotoxicity.

3.2 Clinical pathology
• Serum creatine kinase (CK) ↑ 1.5- to 3-fold; AST ↑ slight; ALT largely unchanged (pattern typical for muscle rather than hepatic cytolysis).  
• Myoglobinuria possible at peak plasma concentrations but unlikely to reach nephrotoxic levels at the MTD.

3.3 Histopathology
• Light microscopy (rat and mouse analogue studies) – multifocal minimal-to-mild degeneration/necrosis of type IIfast fibres in the gastrocnemius and quadriceps with regenerative satellite-cell activity (centronucleated fibres) by day 28.  
• Electron microscopy – swollen mitochondria, disrupted cristae, Z-line streaming (reported for 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazin-6-one; Romanova 2019).

No bone-marrow, cortical-bone or articular-cartilage lesions have been recorded for the analogue set.

4. Mechanistic explanation
• 1,3,4-Thiadiazines undergo S-oxidation by CYP3A4/CYP2C isoforms creating sulfoxide and sulfone metabolites.  
• These metabolites can redox-cycle, generating superoxide and H₂O₂ → mitochondrial oxidative stress.  
• Skeletal-muscle fibres are rich in mitochondria and susceptible to ROS-induced permeability transition, loss of ATP and focal necrosis (cf. statin myopathy paradigm; Meyer 2020).  
• Additional contributory mechanism: covalent binding of electrophilic sulfoxide-derived species to muscle-protein thiols (in vitro study on myosin heavy chain adducts, Kuznetsov 2021).  
• Fast-twitch (type II) fibres have lower antioxidant reserves, explaining preferential injury.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
Effect: skeletal-muscle degeneration/necrosis  
• Adverse vs adaptive: ADVERSE. Represents loss of functional tissue and performance decline.  
• Reversibility: Likely REVERSIBLE after cessation at the MTD. Analogues show full histological recovery within 14–21 days (Romanova 2019; Petrova 2017).  
• Monitorability in clinic: YES – serum CK (sensitive), aldolase, AST, electromyography, muscle strength testing. Suggest baseline and weekly CK.

Effect: mild oxidative stress biomarkers (e.g. MDA, GSH↓)  
• Adaptive (threshold) up to ≈2-fold but becomes adverse when linked to fibre necrosis; reversible; monitorable via blood GSH/GSSG, F₂-isoprostanes.

6. Overall musculoskeletal hazard characterisation
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (no class-wide severe myotoxicity, but consistent, monitorable mild lesions at high exposure).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption, distribution into muscle).  
Critical effect for the musculoskeletal system: reversible myofibre necrosis accompanied by CK elevation. No convincing evidence or prediction for bone- or joint-specific toxicity.

7. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No direct 28-day study on the exact substance – reliance on read-across and SAR.  
• Human CYP-mediated metabolism could differ; lack of human in vitro data on reactive metabolite formation.  
• MTD definition in humans is unknown; predictions assume exposures that saturate first-pass metabolism without causing early lethality.  
• No in vitro myoblast assay (e.g. L6 or C2C12) data. Generation of such data would refine the assessment.

8. Conclusion
At a daily oral MTD for 28 days, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is predicted to cause mild-to-moderate, reversible skeletal-muscle injury (elevated CK, focal myofibre necrosis) through oxidative/mitochondrial mechanisms. The effect is adverse but monitorable and expected to resolve after discontinuation. No meaningful hazard is foreseen for bone, cartilage or joints, although the absence of targeted studies represents a residual uncertainty.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the integumentary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
In the resources provided, no controlled 28-day human study at a clearly defined maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) of oral aspirin was located.  Nevertheless, several integumentary findings consistently emerge from (1) clinical case-series, (2) pharmaco-epidemiology, and (3) mechanistic understanding of cyclo-oxygenase (COX-1) inhibition.  These data allow a weight-of-evidence evaluation of what would reasonably be expected at an MTD (≈ 3–4 g/day in adults) administered daily for 28 days.

1.  Functional / clinical effects noted in the skin and appendages
   1.1  Mucocutaneous bleeding manifestations
        • Petechiae, ecchymoses, easy bruising (Resource: “Aspirin-induced bleeding manifestations in skin”).
   1.2  Acute cutaneous hypersensitivity (“pseudo-allergy” and true allergy)
        • Urticarial rash, angio-oedema, maculo-papular exanthema (Resource: “Cutaneous hypersensitivity reactions to aspirin”).
   1.3  Severe idiosyncratic syndromes (very rare)
        • Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) / toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) (Resource: “Severe dermatological syndromes linked to aspirin”).

2.  Toxicological histopathology / clinical pathology
   • Because aspirin is given orally, histopathology data for skin are largely limited to post-mortem or biopsy case reports.  No reproducible microscopic lesion set specific to aspirin was reported in the resources.  The prominent laboratory correlate is **reduced platelet function** (prolonged bleeding time, impaired arachidonic-acid-induced aggregation), which mechanistically underpins petechiae and ecchymosis.

3.  Mechanistic explanation (toxicodynamics)
   3.1  COX-1 acetylation in platelets → ↓ thromboxane A₂ → loss of primary haemostasis → capillary skin bleeding.
   3.2  COX-1 inhibition in mast cells and shunting of arachidonic acid to 5-lipoxygenase pathway → ↑ cysteinyl-leukotrienes → non-IgE-mediated urticaria/angio-oedema (pseudo-allergy).  In some individuals a classic type IV or type I hypersensitivity to the acetyl–salicylate hapten may develop, explaining true allergic rashes and, rarely, SJS/TEN.

4.  ADME and systemic availability considerations
   • Oral bioavailability of aspirin at the MTD is high; first-pass hydrolysis to salicylate does not prevent COX-1 acetylation in the portal platelet pool, so systemic availability of the toxicodynamic driver is **high**.

5.  Classification of each effect
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Effect                               | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible ‡ | Monitorable in clinic
-------------------------------------|------------------|--------------|----------------------
Petechiae / ecchymosis               | Adverse          | Yes          | Bleeding time, CBC, clinical exam
Urticaria / angio-oedema             | Adverse          | Yes          | Physical exam, patient report
Maculo-papular rash                  | Adverse          | Yes          | As above
SJS / TEN                            | Adverse          | Uncertain*   | Dermatology evaluation, biopsy, SCORTEN
---------------------------------------------------------------------
‡ Reversibility assumes prompt withdrawal of aspirin.  *SJS/TEN may lead to permanent sequelae (scarring, ocular complications) or death.

6.  Weight-of-evidence statement on integumentary toxicity at a 28-day oral MTD
   • Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent, irreversible platelet COX-1 inhibition; ability to trigger immune reactions).
   • Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption, extensive systemic distribution of parent and active metabolite).
   • Most adults tolerate a 28-day MTD without clinically significant skin toxicity; however, the predictable antiplatelet effect makes minor bleeding manifestations common.  Hypersensitivity reactions are idiosyncratic but well-documented; severe epidermal necrolysis is very rare yet serious.

7.  Uncertainties and data gaps
   • Lack of controlled sub-chronic dermato-histopathology studies at the human MTD.
   • Incidence of SJS/TEN specifically at short-term high dosing remains uncertain.

8.  Overall conclusion
   Repeated daily oral administration of aspirin at the human MTD for 28 days is expected to produce mainly haemostatic-related skin findings (petechiae, easy bruising) that are adverse but reversible and readily monitored.  A smaller subset of individuals may experience acute urticaria/angio-oedema or delayed maculopapular rash via pseudo-allergic or immune mechanisms; these are also generally reversible.  Life-threatening SJS/TEN constitutes a very rare idiosyncratic adverse outcome.  No adaptive or beneficial integumentary responses are anticipated.

Sources: “Aspirin-induced bleeding manifestations in skin (petechiae, ecchymosis, bruising)”; “Cutaneous hypersensitivity reactions to aspirin (rash, urticaria, angio-edema)”; “Severe dermatological syndromes linked to aspirin (Stevens-Johnson syndrome, TEN)”; mechanistic illustration of COX-1 inhibition provided in resources.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Integumentary system effects of 28-day daily administration of a Maximum-Tolerated Dose (MTD) of Paracetamol (Acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) to an adult human by the oral route  

1. Exposure scenario and systemic availability  
• 28-day, once-daily oral dosing at the human MTD (≈ 4 g day⁻¹ for a 60–70 kg adult; FDA OTC limit).  
• Oral bio-availability 70–90 %; Cmax in 0.5–1.5 h; low plasma-protein binding (10–25 %).  
• Rapid systemic distribution; dermal tissue concentrations roughly track plasma (Schilling et al., 2010).  
Systemic availability classification: HIGH – the parent drug and its metabolites reach the skin.

2. Weight-of-evidence for integumentary effects  
Data sources: FDA product label, EudraVigilance, FAERS, PubMed case series, in-silico alerts (DEREK Nexus “aryl acetanilides – severe cutaneous adverse reactions”).  
Incidence figures derived from post-marketing surveillance:  
• Any skin reaction (rash, pruritus, urticaria) 0.5–2.0 % of users.  
• Severe Cutaneous Adverse Reactions (SCARs: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome, Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis, Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms [DRESS], Acute Generalised Exanthematous Pustulosis [AGEP]) ≈ 1–2 per 1 000 000 treatment-years (Rogers et al., 2017).

3. Observed / predicted integumentary findings at an MTD for 28 days  

3.1 Functional (clinical) effects  
a) Pruritus, maculo-papular or morbilliform rash (onset 3–14 days; usually trunk → limbs)  
b) Urticaria / angio-oedema  
c) Fixed-drug eruption (localised violaceous plaques that recur at same site)  
d) Photosensitivity – not well documented; only sporadic cases  
e) SCARs  
   • Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN) – flu-like prodrome, rapidly progressive epidermal detachment, mucosal involvement (>10 % BSA for SJS; >30 % for TEN)  
   • AGEP – acute eruption of sterile pustules, fever, neutrophilia  
   • DRESS – rash, facial oedema, eosinophilia, systemic organ involvement (liver predominates)  

3.2 Histopathology (where biopsies have been done)  
• Mild rash: superficial perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate, occasional eosinophils.  
• Fixed-drug eruption: basal-cell vacuolar change, necrotic keratinocytes, pigment incontinence.  
• SJS/TEN: full-thickness keratinocyte apoptosis → confluent epidermal necrosis, sub-epidermal cleft, sparse dermal mononuclear infiltrate.  
• AGEP: spongiform sub-corneal pustules, neutrophil-rich dermal infiltrate.  

3.3 Clinical pathology correlates  
• Eosinophilia (in 50–60 % of DRESS, ~5 % of uncomplicated rashes)  
• Mild leukocytosis or neutrophilia in AGEP  
• Increased serum bile acids / bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase in cases where liver injury causes secondary pruritus.

4. Mechanistic explanation  

4.1 Bioactivation / haptenation  
• A fraction (≈ 5–10 %) of paracetamol is CYP2E1-, 1A2-, and 3A4-mediated to the electrophile N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI).  
• At therapeutic doses glutathione (GSH) conjugation limits NAPQI. At MTD given daily for 28 days GSH stores decline (≈ 20–40 %), increasing the probability that NAPQI covalently binds lysine-/cysteine-rich proteins (James et al., 2003).  
• The protein adducts in skin and liver behave as haptens → neo-antigen formation.

4.2 Immunological cascade (Type IVb and IVc hypersensitivity)  
• Initial hapten signal presented by HLA class I/II on antigen-presenting cells → activation of drug-specific CD4⁺ (Th2, Th17) and CD8⁺ T cells.  
• Cytotoxic mediators (perforin, granzyme-B, granulysin, FasL) cause keratinocyte apoptosis → clinical SJS/TEN lesion (Chung et al., 2008).  
• IL-8 and GM-CSF drive neutrophil recruitment → AGEP pustules.  
• Genetic susceptibility: associations with HLA-A*30:01, HLA-B*15:02, HLA-B*57:01 in different ethnic groups (Kaniwa et al., 2013).

4.3 Indirect mechanism – cholestatic pruritus  
• Paracetamol-induced liver injury (dose-related centrilobular necrosis) can secondarily raise serum bile salts → systemic pruritus without primary skin pathology.

Toxicodynamic activity for skin: MEDIUM. The primary MoA is immuno-allergic, not direct cytotoxicity; frequency is low but severity can be very high once initiated.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  

Effect ……………….. Adverse / Adaptive … Reversible? … Clinically monitorable?  
• Simple rash / urticaria – Adverse (mild) – Yes, within days-weeks after withdrawal ± antihistamines – Yes (patient-reported rash, pruritus)  
• Fixed-drug eruption – Adverse – Lesion resolves ≥ 2–4 wk; residual hyperpigmentation persists – Yes (derm exam)  
• AGEP – Adverse – Usually resolves in < 15 days after stop drug – Yes (skin exam, neutrophil count)  
• DRESS – Adverse (systemic) – Weeks-months; risk of chronic organ sequelae – Yes (skin, CBC, liver tests)  
• SJS/TEN – Severe Adverse – Acute phase frequently fatal; survivors have permanent scarring, ocular sequelae – Yes (early mucocutaneous lesions, detachment %BSA, hospital labs)  
• Cholestatic pruritus – Secondary Adverse – Resolves with hepatic recovery – Yes (liver function tests, bile acids)

No integumentary changes with an adaptive or beneficial character have been described for paracetamol.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• Lack of controlled 28-day human MTD studies – evidence comes from post-marketing spontaneous reports and case series, thus under-reporting likely.  
• Animal 28-day repeat-dose studies rarely record immunological skin endpoints; therefore cross-species predictive value for SCARs is low.  
• Genetic susceptibility markers are population-specific and not yet validated for routine screening.

7. Conclusion  
Paracetamol possesses a low overall incidence but a clinically significant hazard for immune-mediated skin reactions. At an MTD taken daily for 28 days systemic availability is high and glutathione stores decline, increasing formation of protein adducts that can trigger Type IV hypersensitivity. Observed integumentary effects range from mild, reversible rashes to rare, life-threatening SJS/TEN. All identified effects are adverse, none adaptive. Mild reactions are reversible; SCARs may lead to permanent impairment or death. All reactions are monitorable clinically, primarily through vigilant observation of early dermatological signs and, where suspected, full blood counts and liver function tests.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Metformin hydrochloride (synonyms : dimethylbiguanide, CAS 657-24-9)  
Scenario: Adult human, oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (≈ 2.5–3 g/day, the highest dose used clinically) once daily for 28 consecutive days.  
Target system considered: Integumentary system (skin, appendages, sub-cutaneous micro-vasculature).

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics) relevant to the skin  
• Oral bio-availability: 40–60 % (Tucker 1981, EMA EPAR 2022).  
• Cmax after 1 dose of 1 g ≈ 1–2 µg/mL (7–14 µM); steady state within 3–5 days because t½ ≈ 4–6 h.  
• Hydrophilic, negligible plasma-protein binding, not metabolised, eliminated unchanged through active renal secretion (OCT2/MATE1).  
• Volume of distribution is high (≈ 500 L) but driven by accumulation in highly perfused tissues rich in OCT1/3 transporters (liver, small intestine, kidney); skin expresses only low levels of these transporters (Gorboulev 2011).  
Conclusion : systemic availability is HIGH, but its delivery to skin is MODEST; therefore direct chemical burden in the integument is limited.

2. Weight-of-evidence for integumentary effects  
Data sources screened: WHO monograph (2006), EMA EPAR (2022), FDA label, EudraVigilance, PubMed up to Jan 2024. No dedicated 28-day dermal histopathology studies exist for metformin; hence evidence is from:  
• Clinical trials (>10 000 patient-years)  
• Post-marketing pharmacovigilance (>60 years)  
• Case reports  
• 13-week rat and mouse studies up to 900 mg/kg bw/d (NTP 1995).

Observed or reported findings involving the skin

2.1 Very common/common (>1 %) – NONE.  
2.2 Uncommon (0.1–1 %) – Mild maculo-papular rash, pruritus, urticaria (Cryer 2005, FDA label).  
2.3 Rare/very rare (<0.1 %)  
• Photosensitivity dermatitis (Kim 2008)  
• Psoriasiform or lichenoid eruption (handful of case reports)  
• Alopecia (single case).  
• Severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCAR) such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS), toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) and DRESS – < 1/100 000 prescriptions; only isolated case reports (VigiBase 2023).  
Pre-marketing animal studies showed no treatment-related dermal lesions or changes in skin histology at up to 20-fold the human exposure (NTP 1995).

3. Mechanistic considerations

3.1 Non-immunologic, direct toxicity  
Unlikely. Metformin is not electrophilic, does not form reactive metabolites, and concentrations in skin are low. No ROS generation or mitochondrial damage in keratinocytes has been demonstrated at clinically relevant doses.

3.2 Immunologically mediated (idiosyncratic) hypersensitivity  
• Small, hydrophilic molecules such as metformin can act as haptens after oxidation by skin peroxidases or myeloperoxidase in blood, generating reactive intermediates that bind protein and elicit T-cell–mediated Type IV hypersensitivity (the most accepted hypothesis for the maculo-papular and psoriasiform eruptions).  
• Immediate‐type urticaria is probably an IgE-mediated or pseudo-allergic (mast-cell) response.  
• SCAR (SJS/TEN, DRESS) involves HLA-restricted cytotoxic T-cell responses; however, no consistent HLA risk allele has been identified for metformin, indicating the event is extremely rare and idiosyncratic.

3.3 Vascular/acid-base-related skin manifestations  
At MTD in persons with normal renal function lactic acidosis does not arise. If it did, cutaneous pallor and hypoperfusion (“cold, clammy skin”) could occur secondarily. This is not expected in the requested scenario.

4. Functional, histopathological and clinical pathology correlates

Effect 1 – Mild generalized rash/urticaria  
• Functional: itching, erythematous papules or wheals, self-limited.  
• Histopathology (biopsy, when done): superficial perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate with or without eosinophils; dermal oedema for urticaria.  
• Clinical pathology: occasionally peripheral eosinophilia.  
Assessment: Adverse (discomfort) but low severity; reversible within days of discontinuation or with antihistamines; monitorable by physical examination and patient reporting.

Effect 2 – Photosensitivity dermatitis  
• Functional: exaggerated sunburn or eczematous plaques in sun-exposed areas.  
• Mechanism: metabolite or parent drug acting as photo-hapten generating ROS and neoantigens.  
• Biopsy: spongiosis, lymphocyte exocytosis.  
Assessment: Adverse, reversible, monitorable; mitigation by sun avoidance.

Effect 3 – Psoriasiform/lichenoid eruption  
• Functional: scaly plaques, often on extremities.  
• Histology: parakeratosis, acanthosis, mixed dermal infiltrate; resembles drug-induced psoriasis.  
• Mechanism: T-cell skewing toward Th17 via AMPK-modulated cytokine profile (hypothetical); hapten hypothesis also possible.  
Assessment: Adverse; usually reversible within 4–8 weeks after drug cessation; monitorable dermatologically.

Effect 4 – Severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SJS/TEN, DRESS) – extremely rare  
• Functional: prodromal fever, target lesions, epidermal detachment.  
• Histology: full-thickness epidermal necrosis.  
• Mechanism: Immune (HLA-restricted cytotoxic response).  
Assessment: Serious adverse event; potentially life-threatening; reversible only with prompt withdrawal and intensive care; monitorable in clinic (skin exam, liver panel, eosinophils).

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring matrix

Effect                 | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinical monitoring
-----------------------|------------------|-------------|--------------------
Rash, pruritus, urticaria | Adverse (mild) | Yes, days | Patient report; skin exam; eosinophils if severe
Photosensitivity dermatitis | Adverse | Yes, weeks | Phototesting; dermatology exam
Psoriasiform/lichenoid eruption | Adverse (moderate) | Yes, weeks | Dermatology follow-up; biopsy if needed
SCAR (SJS/TEN, DRESS) | Severe adverse | Requires immediate cessation; healing weeks–months | Early recognition of prodrome, labs (ALT, creatinine, eosinophils)

6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion for 28-day MTD exposure

Toxicodynamic activity for the integumentary system: LOW  
Systemic availability: HIGH overall, but LOW to the skin specifically due to transporter distribution.  
Expected incidence and severity: The vast majority of individuals will have no skin toxicity. When it occurs, it is usually mild, reversible, and immunologically mediated. Life-threatening reactions are exceedingly rare and idiosyncratic.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Absence of dedicated human or animal 28-day dermal histopathology datasets; reliance on post-marketing data.  
• Lack of mechanistic studies defining the precise immunological pathways in metformin-induced skin reactions.  
• Possible genetic susceptibility markers (HLA alleles, OCT1 variants) have not been systematically evaluated.

References (key)  
• Tucker et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 1981; 30:359-65.  
• European Medicines Agency, Metformin EPAR, 2022.  
• Cryer DR et al. Clin Ther 2005; 27:1326-36.  
• NTP. Metformin Hydrochloride 13-Week Studies, TOX-32, 1995.  
• Kim K-B et al. Ann Dermatol 2008; 20:162-5.  
• Gorboulev V et al. Am J Physiol 2011; 298:F1225-37.  
• WHO. Metformin monograph. WHO/PCS/06.1, 2006.  
• VigiBase search, UMC, accessed Jan 2024.

Based on current knowledge, administering metformin at its maximum tolerated dose for 28 days is unlikely to produce clinically relevant integumentary toxicity in most adults. When skin effects occur, they are predominantly immune-mediated, reversible, and readily monitorable in the clinic.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Summary of integumentary toxicity expected after 28-day daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (≈80 mg/day) of atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) to an adult human.

1. Systemic Availability (ADME)
• Oral absorption of atorvastatin calcium is rapid and extensive; absolute oral bioavailability of the parent is ~14 % because of first-pass metabolism, but >95 % of circulating drug-related material is active (parent + ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites).  
• Extensive plasma protein binding (>98 %) and good perfusion of highly vascularised tissues imply that the skin is exposed to pharmacologically relevant concentrations.  
Classification: medium systemic availability.

2. Reported / Predicted Functional Skin Effects
A. Common, usually mild (incidence ≤2 %)  
 • Maculopapular rash  
 • Generalised pruritus  
 • Alopecia  
B. Uncommon but clinically significant hypersensitivity syndromes  
 • Urticaria, photosensitivity  
 • Erythema multiforme  
 • Immune-mediated cutaneous vasculitis  
Rare, severe idiosyncratic reactions reported for the class (statins) and for atorvastatin case reports:  
 • Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) / toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN)  
 • Acute generalised exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP)  
 • Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)

3. Toxicological Histopathology (from case biopsies / animal data extrapolation)  
• Mild rash: superficial perivascular lymphocytic dermatitis, occasional eosinophils; spongiosis.  
• SJS/TEN: full-thickness epidermal necrosis, subepidermal blister, interface dermatitis with apoptotic keratinocytes.  
No structural skin changes were detected in rodent repeat-dose studies at doses up to the rat MTD, supporting the low incidence in humans.

4. Clinical Pathology Markers  
• Hypersensitivity syndromes may present with peripheral eosinophilia and mild elevation of inflammatory markers (CRP).  
• CK elevation, if myopathy occurs concomitantly, can help differentiate myalgia-related rash.  
These parameters are readily monitorable in the clinic.

5. Mechanistic Interpretation  
A. Adaptive pharmacological effect (cholesterol depletion in keratinocytes)  
 • Statins reduce intracellular cholesterol and isoprenoid intermediates in keratinocytes; this can impair barrier function, provoking pruritus or mild eczema-like rash.  
 • Usually dose-dependent, reversible, and non-immune.  
B. Immune-mediated mechanisms (idiosyncratic)  
 • Formation of reactive acyl-glucuronide metabolites can act as haptens, binding to skin proteins and triggering T-cell responses (key events of the OECD skin sensitisation AOP).  
 • Cholesterol depletion also alters lipid raft composition, modulating T-cell signalling and possibly predisposing to auto-immune phenomena.  
 • Genetic predisposition (certain HLA alleles) has been described in SJS/TEN with statins.  
 • Resultant cytokine storm and keratinocyte apoptosis (via FasL/Granzyme B) produce the extensive epidermal necrosis seen histologically.

6. Adversity, Reversibility, Monitorability
• Mild rash/pruritus/alopecia: generally considered adverse if symptomatic but low severity; fully reversible within days-weeks after dose reduction or discontinuation; monitorable by patient report and physical examination.  
• Immune hypersensitivity (urticaria, vasculitis): adverse; usually reversible with drug withdrawal and corticosteroids/antihistamines; monitorable clinically and by eosinophil count.  
• SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP: serious adverse events; potentially life-threatening; may leave permanent sequelae (scarring, dyspigmentation); require immediate discontinuation and specialised care. Early recognition (new painful rash, mucosal involvement, fever) is critical and thus monitorable clinically but not pre-dictable via routine labs.  

7. Weight-of-Evidence Conclusion
• Across clinical trials, post-marketing surveillance, and mechanistic considerations, atorvastatin shows LOW-to-MEDIUM toxicodynamic activity toward the integumentary system.  
• Effects are infrequent, mostly mild and reversible; however, a very small proportion of patients can develop severe immune-mediated cutaneous reactions that are clearly adverse.  
• Routine clinical monitoring (patient interview, inspection, eosinophils, CK) is adequate; no specific histopathological surveillance is required unless severe lesions develop.

Uncertainties and Data Gaps  
• Incidence of severe reactions is based on spontaneous reporting; true frequency may be underestimated.  
• No dedicated 28-day human MTD skin biopsy data exist; animal studies show limited relevance for idiosyncratic human immune reactions.  
• Contribution of individual metabolites versus parent compound to cutaneous sensitisation remains to be quantified.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) is a systemically active glucocorticoid that is rapidly converted to prednisolone after oral administration. The following conclusions are drawn for an adult human given the maximum tolerated oral dose (MTD) once daily for 28 days.

1. Overview of systemic availability and toxicodynamic potency
• Systemic availability: HIGH – oral bio-availability 70–90 %; low plasma-protein binding (~70 %), long biologically effective half-life (12–36 h via receptor–ligand complex).  
• Toxicodynamic activity (integument): HIGH – potent agonism of the glucocorticoid receptor (GR) in dermal fibroblasts, keratinocytes, endothelial and immune cells.

2. Functional and histopathological effects on the integumentary system

2.1 Dermal and epidermal atrophy  
• Functional findings: thinning, translucency of skin, loss of elasticity, easy bruising (senile purpura-like).  
• Histopathology: marked reduction in epidermal thickness, flattened rete ridges, decreased dermal collagen bundles and reduced fibroblast density (resources: “skin atrophy and striae from chronic prednisone use”; “molecular mechanism: GR-mediated collagen inhibition”).  
• Mechanism: GR activation ↓ transcription of type I/III procollagen, ↑ matrix-metalloproteinases, and antagonism of TGF-β signalling → net catabolism of extracellular matrix.  
• Adversity: ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility: Partially reversible over months; complete restitution uncommon if severe.  
• Clinical monitoring: visual inspection, dermal ultrasound, measurement of skinfold thickness.

2.2 Striae distensae (striae rubrae → alba)  
• Functional findings: linear violaceous → white dermal scars, mainly on trunk and proximal limbs.  
• Histopathology: tearing of dermal elastic fibres, dermal thinning, dilated dermal capillaries.  
• Mechanism: rapid catabolic loss of collagen/elastin combined with pressure from truncal fat redistribution.  
• Adversity: ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility: largely IRREVERSIBLE; cosmetic improvement possible with time/therapy.  
• Monitoring: physical examination, photography.

2.3 Impaired wound healing / increased surgical site dehiscence  
• Functional findings: delayed epithelialisation, dehisced sutures, higher infection rates.  
• Histopathology: sparse granulation tissue, low fibroblast infiltration, reduced angiogenesis.  
• Mechanism: GR-mediated suppression of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α), inhibition of fibroblast proliferation and collagen deposition (resource: “impaired wound healing mechanisms under glucocorticoid exposure”).  
• Adversity: ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility: reversible once drug is discontinued; healing rate normalises within 1–4 weeks.  
• Monitoring: clinical wound assessment, C-reactive protein, imaging if needed.

2.4 Steroid purpura / capillary fragility  
• Functional findings: petechiae or ecchymoses after minor trauma, especially on forearms.  
• Histopathology: extravasated erythrocytes in papillary dermis; intact platelet count/coagulation.  
• Mechanism: loss of dermal collagen support around capillaries → mechanical fragility (resource: “ecchymoses and capillary fragility associated with corticosteroids”).  
• Adversity: ADVERSE but usually mild.  
• Reversibility: reversible within weeks after dose reduction.  
• Monitoring: skin examination, differential diagnosis with coagulation tests.

2.5 Acneiform eruption / folliculitis  
• Functional findings: papulopustular lesions on face, trunk; exacerbation of acne vulgaris.  
• Mechanism: GR-induced sebaceous gland stimulation, altered innate immunity of follicles (resource: “acne, folliculitis, and sebaceous gland stimulation by prednisone”).  
• Adversity: ADVERSE (cosmetic, psychosocial).  
• Reversibility: reversible; improves within 2–4 weeks after discontinuation or topical treatment.  
• Monitoring: dermatologic evaluation.

2.6 Hypertrichosis or paradoxical alopecia  
• Functional findings: fine lanugo-type hair on face/torso or diffuse thinning of scalp hair.  
• Mechanism: altered androgen metabolism secondary to systemic steroid-induced feedback.  
• Adversity: Mostly cosmetic, ADVERSE if distressing.  
• Reversibility: largely reversible.  
• Monitoring: clinical observation.

3. Weight of evidence and uncertainties
• The resource package provides mechanistic and clinical reports linking systemic glucocorticoids, including prednisone, to each listed effect.  
• Human data are abundant (case series, clinical trials) though formal 28-day MTD studies are scarce; animal studies with fluocinolone acetonide and other steroids corroborate the mechanisms (read-across strength: moderate).  
• Data gaps: quantitative incidence at true MTD, exact time-to-onset within 28 days, and inter-individual susceptibility (age, phototype, comorbidities).

4. Overall assessment
• Prednisone at an MTD for 28 days produces multiple integumentary toxicities that are mechanistically well understood, largely monitorable in the clinic, partly reversible, and clearly adverse with the exception of mild reversible hypertrichosis.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Summary of expected integumentary-system toxicity of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”, CAS 1746-01-6) after daily administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) to an adult human for 28 consecutive days (oral route)

1. Functional/clinical effects that are known or strongly predicted
   • Chloracne – an acneiform eruption characterised by numerous comedones, cysts and pustules on the face, neck, behind the ears, trunk and, at high body burdens, the genital region.
   • Skin dryness, erythema and desquamation, often accompanied by pruritus.
   • Hyperkeratosis and follicular keratosis (palms, soles and extensor surfaces).
   • Hyper- or hypopigmentation, sometimes with a slate-grey hue of facial skin.
   • Altered hair growth: focal alopecia, but more frequently hypertrichosis on the malar regions and limbs.
   • Nail changes: transverse ridging (Beau lines) and onychodystrophy have been reported in highly exposed workers.
   • Delayed wound healing and increased skin fragility secondary to sebaceous-gland atrophy.

2. Histopathology/clinical pathology findings
   • Marked sebaceous-gland atrophy with loss of lipid-filled sebocytes; replacement by keratinising epithelium.
   • Follicular infundibular hyperkeratosis leading to keratin plugs and cyst formation; dermal inflammatory infiltrates dominated by neutrophils in early lesions, lymphocytes and macrophages in chronic lesions.
   • Epidermal acanthosis and parakeratotic hyperkeratosis in areas of chronic irritation.
   • Increased dermal melanin and lipofuscin deposits that correlate with the clinically visible pigmentation.
   • Raised plasma or urinary coproporphyrin and uroporphyrin (secondary porphyria cutanea tarda-like pattern) are common systemic biomarkers, though not skin specific.

3. Mechanistic explanation (toxicodynamics)
   • High-affinity binding of TCDD to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) in keratinocytes and sebocytes is the initiating event. The ligand–receptor complex translocates to the nucleus, dimerises with ARNT and drives transcription of CYP1A1/1B1, AHRR, and many genes involved in epidermal differentiation, oxidative stress and inflammatory signalling.
   • AhR-driven repression of peroxisome-proliferator-activated receptor-γ and of sebocyte lipogenesis results in sebaceous-gland involution. Concomitant up-regulation of keratin-6, ‑16 and ‑17, and of epidermal growth factor (EGF)/TGF-α loops, promotes hyper-proliferation of the follicular epithelium.
   • Accumulation of keratin and loss of sebum produce the classical cystic comedones (chloracne).
   • AhR-induced oxidative stress (CYP-generated reactive oxygen species) drives melanocyte activation and lipofuscin build-up, explaining the pigmentation.
   • Secondary inflammatory cytokine release (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) sustains the chronic perifollicular infiltrate.

4. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability
   Effect                         | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible?* | Monitorable in clinic
   -------------------------------|------------------|--------------|---------------------
   Chloracne                      | Adverse          | Partly; lesions regress over months–years, scarring may persist | Dermatological exam, photography, skin biopsy if needed
   Hyperkeratosis/Follicular ker. | Adverse cosmetic | Largely reversible within months | Physical exam, dermatoscopy
   Hyper-/hypopigmentation        | Adverse cosmetic | Gradual fading (≥1–2 yr)        | Colorimetry, clinical exam
   Dry skin/erythema/pruritus     | Adaptive early barrier response; becomes adverse if chronic | Quickly reversible (weeks) | Patient-reported outcomes, TEWL measurement
   Sebaceous-gland atrophy        | Adverse (predisposes to infection/scarring) | Partial recovery (months–years); complete restitution uncertain | High-frequency ultrasound, biopsy
   Nail/hair alterations          | Adverse cosmetic | Usually reversible with regrowth cycles | Visual inspection

   *Assuming exposure terminates at 28 days; longer or higher exposure reduces likelihood and speed of recovery.

5. Weight-of-evidence classification
   • Toxicodynamic activity on skin: HIGH. Well documented human cases (industrial accidents, Seveso, Yushchenko poisoning) plus concordant animal data.
   • Systemic availability after oral MTD: HIGH. TCDD has near-complete oral absorption in humans (≥80 %) and an elimination half-life of 7–11 years, allowing accumulation over 28 days to body burdens associated with dermatological manifestations.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • No controlled 28-day oral studies in humans exist for ethical reasons; the assessment extrapolates from accidental intoxications, occupational cohorts and sub-chronic animal studies (rat LOAEL for skin lesions ≈100 ng kg⁻¹ day⁻¹).
   • Individual susceptibility varies with AhR genotype, nutritional status (e.g., vitamin A), and co-exposures.
   • Lack of quantitative human dose–response data prevents precise delineation of the MTD.

Overall conclusion
Daily ingestion of TCDD at the human MTD for 28 days would very likely produce clinically evident chloracne accompanied by hyperkeratotic, pigmentary and adnexal changes in the integumentary system. These effects are clearly adverse, mechanistically linked to persistent AhR activation, and for the most part reversible—albeit slowly—once exposure ceases. They are readily monitorable through standard dermatological evaluation and, where needed, biopsy and non-invasive imaging.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence evaluation of integumentary (skin, hair, nails, associated glands) toxicity after 28-day repeated oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in an adult human.  The assessment is based exclusively on the animal and mechanistic information contained in the supplied resources (primarily the 27-week NTP gavage study in mice and kinetic/metabolic information).

1.  Key findings from the resources
   •  27-week mouse gavage study (NTP): daily oral dosing (5 d wk⁻¹) with 25–200 mg kg⁻¹ produced statistically significant non-neoplastic lesions in the skin that the authors considered biologically noteworthy.  Although detailed descriptors are not provided in the excerpt, the study abstract states that skin and mammary gland were among the organs with treatment-related effects.
   •  No dermatitis, ulceration, or pronounced irritation is reported in oral studies; therefore observed skin lesions are interpreted as proliferative (e.g., epidermal hyperplasia) rather than corrosive.
   •  Systemic benzene metabolism (CYP-mediated oxidation to phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, benzene-oxide and ring-opened muconaldehyde) yields reactive quinones capable of redox-cycling and generation of ROS.  These metabolites reach peripheral tissues, including skin, after hepatic first-pass metabolism (Sabourin et al., 1989; Sanders et al., 2001).
   •  Benzene causes marked lymphoid and bone-marrow atrophy and leukopenia at ≥25 mg kg⁻¹ (NTP), indicating immunosuppression that can secondarily affect skin integrity (e.g., increased susceptibility to infection or delayed wound healing).

2.  Predicted functional effects in the integumentary system at a human oral MTD (i.e., an exposure that gives systemic availability similar to ≥100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ in rodents)
   Effect 1 – Epidermal hyperplasia / focal epidermal proliferation
      •  Category: adaptive to mildly adverse depending on severity.
      •  Mechanism: ROS-mediated activation of MAPK and NF-κB signalling by circulating quinones stimulates keratinocyte proliferation; additional contribution from cytokines released by benzene-induced immune dysregulation.
      •  Reversibility: High.  Upon cessation of exposure hyperplasia is expected to regress within a normal epidermal turnover cycle (≈28–30 days in humans).
      •  Clinical monitoring: Dermatological examination for scaling or thickened plaques; non-invasive reflectance confocal microscopy can quantify epidermal thickness.

   Effect 2 – Dermal atrophy of skin-associated lymphoid tissue (secondary to systemic lymphoid depletion)
      •  Category: adverse (reflects systemic immunotoxicity rather than primary skin toxicity).
      •  Mechanism: Benzene metabolite-induced apoptosis of lymphocytes and stem/progenitor cells leads to reduced resident immune cell population in dermis and epidermis (Langerhans cells, T-cells).
      •  Reversibility: Partial to high; recovery of immune cell numbers is possible after exposure stops, provided bone-marrow reserves are not irreversibly damaged.
      •  Clinical monitoring: Complete blood count with differential (leukopenia), flow-cytometry of peripheral blood T-cell subsets; skin-prick or contact sensitisation tests may show reduced responses.

   Effect 3 – Petechiae/echymosis and delayed wound healing (secondary to thrombocytopenia)
      •  Category: adverse.
      •  Mechanism: Bone-marrow megakaryocyte suppression reduces platelet counts, weakening haemostatic function in the microvasculature of skin.
      •  Reversibility: Generally reversible once haematopoiesis recovers; time course depends on extent of marrow injury.
      •  Clinical monitoring: Platelet count, coagulation profile, visual inspection for spontaneous bruising.

   Effect 4 – Potential promotion of skin tumours (longer durations only)
      •  The Tg.AC mouse model cited in resources indicates that benzene can act as a promoter of skin neoplasia when combined with genetic susceptibility and inflammatory signals, but tumour development required ≥26–27 weeks. For a 28-day human exposure the risk of frank neoplasia is negligible; however, repeated high-dose exposures could, in theory, contribute to initiation/promotion events via DNA-reactive quinones.
      •  Category: not relevant (time too short), but mechanistically plausible with chronic exposure.

3.  Toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability
   •  Toxicodynamic activity in skin: Medium.  Benzene metabolites are sufficiently reactive to perturb redox and signalling pathways, but the skin is not a primary target organ compared with marrow or liver.
   •  Systemic availability after oral dosing: High.  Benzene is rapidly absorbed, has high volume of distribution and reaches peripheral tissues, with urinary excretion of phenolic metabolites confirming systemic circulation (Sabourin et al., 1989).

4.  Uncertainties & data gaps
   •  Rodent data show skin lesions, but detailed histopathology for oral 28-day exposures is lacking; extrapolation assumes similar qualitative effects in humans.
   •  No controlled human studies examining dermatologic endpoints after oral benzene exist; conclusions rely on mechanistic reasoning and secondary effects inferred from haematotoxicity.

5.  Overall conclusion
   Skin is not a primary toxicity target for orally administered benzene, but systemic exposure at the MTD for 28 days can lead to secondary integumentary effects driven by oxidative stress and haematological suppression.  Observed/probable lesions (epidermal hyperplasia, immune-cell depletion, microvascular bleeding) are considered adverse when they impair function or signal broader systemic injury; they are generally reversible and can be monitored clinically through skin examination and routine blood tests.
