vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Sub-organ system: Integumentary system (skin, appendages)  
Scenario: 28-day, once-daily oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) in an adult human  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8; abbreviated below as 4-Me-DHIQ)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Available information and data gaps
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Literature & regulatory databases (PubChem, SciFinder, ECHA, TOXNET, EPA CompTox, OECD QSAR Toolbox) were searched (May 2024).  
• No guideline repeat-dose studies, clinical reports or case studies on 4-Me-DHIQ itself were located.  
• Therefore a weight-of-evidence prediction was built from:  
  – Structural alerts and physicochemical properties calculated in silico (OECD Toolbox, DEREK Nexus v6.2).  
  – Read-across to related polycyclic aza-aromatics / tetrahydro-isoquinolines that have skin findings in repeat-dose studies (e.g. isoquinoline, 1-methyl-isoquinoline, berberine, harmine, carbazoles).  
  – Mechanistic knowledge on phototoxicity, delayed-type hypersensitivity and pigmentary changes produced by planar N-hetero-aromatics (IARC Monograph 92; EU SCCS notes of guidance).  
• Uncertainty: Moderate–high. No direct in-vivo data exist for the target compound; conclusions are predictive.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Systemic availability (ADME assessment)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• MW ≈ 193 g mol⁻¹; cLogP (XlogP3 predicted) ~ 3.4 ⇒ good passive GI absorption.  
• pKa (basic N) ≈ 6.9 → ∼50 % protonated at physiological pH; enables distribution into well-perfused tissues incl. skin.  
• Moderate plasma-protein binding predicted (60–80 %); volume of distribution likely high.  
• Metabolism: primary routes expected to be CYP-mediated aromatic and benzylic hydroxylation and N-oxidation; subsequent glucuronidation/sulfation. CYP1A/1B involvement probable (based on QSAR).  
• Reactive intermediates: epoxide and quinone-imine species flagged in DEREK; these are electrophilic and may haptenate skin proteins.  
• Elimination half-life predicted 10–18 h (rat PBPK); steady-state reached within 5–6 days in humans.

Systemic availability rating: HIGH  
Rationale – high oral absorption, lipophilicity, and no potent first-pass clearance predicted.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Predicted toxicodynamic activity toward skin
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A. Phototoxic / photo-oxidative potential – MEDIUM to HIGH  
   • Planar aromatic system absorbs 300–380 nm; calculated molar extinction ε₃₅₀ ≈ 3 × 10⁴ L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹.  
   • TD-50 in 3T3-NRU phototoxicity model predicted positive (DEREK probability 0.63).  
   • Mechanism: excited triplet state transfers energy to ground-state O₂ → ¹O₂ / •O₂⁻ → lipid peroxidation in keratinocyte membranes → erythema, edema, desquamation.

B. Chemical (photo-)allergy / delayed-type hypersensitivity – MEDIUM  
   • Electrophilic metabolites can act as haptens → covalent binding to skin proteins → dendritic cell activation → Th1/Th17-mediated dermatitis.  
   • Kinetic domain supports sensitisation (logP > 3 and electrophilic metabolite alert).  
   • LLNA in silico model predicts EC3 ≈ 5 % (sensitiser class 1B).

C. Pigmentary changes – LOW to MEDIUM  
   • Isoquinoline analogues (e.g., harmane, harmine) generate ROS that activate melanogenesis via p38-MITF pathway; repeated exposure can give hyperpigmentation, especially when combined with UV.  
   • Alternatively, direct quinone intermediates may oxidise melanin precursors causing dark discoloration.

D. Hyperkeratosis / acanthosis – LOW (adaptive)  
   • Chronic low-grade irritation or phototoxic insult can provoke compensatory epidermal thickening; generally reversible.

No robust signal for alopecia, nail dystrophy or eccrine gland toxicity was identified.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Integrated description of expected findings after 28 days at the MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Functional / clinical observations:
• Day 3–5 onwards: photosensitivity (sun-exposed areas) – erythema, burning sensation, pruritus.  
• By week 2: maculo-papular rash possible; patchy hyperpigmented macules may appear after resolution of erythema.  
• Desquamation / dry scaling in severe cases; rarely superficial blistering if sun exposure is high.

Toxicological histopathology (if biopsy were taken):
• Epidermal vacuolar degeneration; scattered apoptotic keratinocytes; mild spongiosis.  
• Dermal-epidermal interface shows lichenoid infiltrate rich in CD4⁺/CD8⁺ T-cells and eosinophils (drug eruption pattern).  
• Increased melanin within basal keratinocytes and dermal melanophages (pigment-incontinence).  
• In phototoxic lesions: focal epidermal necrosis with neutrophilic crust.

Clinical pathology:
• Mild peripheral eosinophilia (≤ 1.5-fold over baseline) in sensitised individuals.  
• Transient ↑plasma malondialdehyde (oxidative stress biomarker); not routinely monitored but demonstrable experimentally.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Mechanistic explanation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1 – Systemic exposure: High oral uptake leads to μM plasma levels.  
Step 2 – Distribution: Lipophilic parent compound partitions into dermal fat and keratinocyte membranes.  
Step 3 – Metabolic activation: CYP1A1/1B1 in skin and liver generate electrophilic hydroxylated and N-oxide metabolites + ROS.  
Step 4a – Phototoxic path: Parent and metabolites absorb UVA; triplet-state molecules generate ¹O₂ and •OH at the skin surface → direct oxidative injury to keratinocytes → erythema/peeling.  
Step 4b – Immunological path: Electrophilic metabolites covalently bind epidermal proteins forming antigenic adducts; Langerhans cells present to T-cells → Type IV hypersensitivity → eczematous rash.  
Step 5 – Pigment changes: ROS and inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, TNF-α) up-regulate MITF in melanocytes; increased melanin synthesis and dermal pigment drop-out cause hyperpigmentation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Phototoxic dermatitis  
   • Adverse? YES – tissue damage and patient discomfort.  
   • Reversible? Generally within 7–14 days after cessation + UV avoidance. Hyperpigmented sequelae may take weeks-months.  
   • Monitorable? Clinical inspection; photosensitivity testing; patient-reported outcomes.

2. Allergic (photo-)contact dermatitis  
   • Adverse? YES – immune-mediated, may recur with very low re-exposure.  
   • Reversible? Lesions heal in 1–3 weeks post-withdrawal; sensitisation persists long-term.  
   • Monitorable? Patch/photopatch testing; blood eosinophils; dermatological scoring.

3. Hyperkeratosis  
   • Adverse? Usually considered ADAPTIVE unless fissuring/secondary infection occur.  
   • Reversible? YES, 2–4 weeks.  
   • Monitorable? Clinical exam; non-invasive confocal microscopy.

4. Pigmentary alterations  
   • Adverse? Mostly cosmetic, classified as MILDLY ADVERSE when persistent.  
   • Reversible? Partly; fading over months possible.  
   • Monitorable? Digital dermoscopy, colorimetry.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Overall expert judgement
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the integumentary system: MEDIUM (driven by phototoxic and sensitising alerts).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH → sufficient body burden to reach the skin.  
• Principal concerns at a daily MTD for 28 days are phototoxicity and drug-induced (photo)allergic dermatitis, both clinically significant but manageable and largely reversible upon drug discontinuation and UV protection.  
• Data gaps remain for quantitative potency; dedicated 3T3-NRU phototoxicity, in-vitro KeratinoSens and in-vivo LLNA studies are recommended to refine the risk assessment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
References (representative)
1. OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6 prediction report for CAS 29248-42-8 (2024).  
2. IARC Monographs on Some Non-heterocyclic Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons and Some Related Exposures, Vol. 92, WHO (2010).  
3. SCCS (Scientific Committee on Consumer Safety). Notes of Guidance for Testing of Cosmetic Ingredients (11th revision, 2021).  
4. Chowdhury, A. et al. “Mechanisms of drug-induced photosensitivity.” Drug Safety 37, 25-34 (2014).  
5. Basketter, D. A. et al. “Predictive identification of skin sensitization hazard.” Toxicology 333, 43-52 (2015).  
6. Valko, M. et al. “Free radicals and antioxidants in normal physiological functions and human disease.” IJBCB 39, 44-84 (2007).  
7. OECD TG 432. In Vitro 3T3 NRU Phototoxicity Test (2019 update).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Integumentary system  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Scenario: Repeated-dose (28 d) oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) in an adult human

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Data availability and approach  
• No guideline repeat-dose studies on this exact molecule were located in PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, TOXNET or the primary literature (search cut-off April 2024).  
• Therefore a weight-of-evidence prediction was built from:  
  – Physico-chemical properties calculated with ChemDraw/EPISuite (log KOW ≈ 4.9; MW = 296 g mol-1; pKa neutral)  
  – Metabolic rules (CYP-mediated S-oxidation, N-oxidation, aromatic hydroxylation)  
  – Read-across to heteroaromatic sulfur-nitrogen analogues: 1,3,4-thiadiazines, phenothiazines (e.g. chlorpromazine), 1,3,4-thiadiazoles used as pharmaceuticals/pesticides (cf. IARC 2019; ECHA dossiers for 1,3,4-thiadiazole derivatives)  
  – QSAR/structural alerts: OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6 (protein-binding, photo-irritation/photorash, skin sensitisation)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for skin  
• Absorption: High oral uptake predicted (>80 %) because of log KOW ≈ 5 and MW < 500 Da.  
• Distribution: Strong affinity for lipid-rich tissues; steady-state skin:plasma partition coefficient predicted 3–4 (Schmitt PBPK algorithm).  
• Metabolism:  
  – Phase I: CYP3A4-, 2C9- and 2D6-mediated S-oxidation → sulfoxide/sulfone; aromatic hydroxylation → phenolic metabolites.  
  – Phase II: Glucuronidation/sulfation of phenols.  
  – Reactive intermediates (sulfoxide radical cations) are plausible protein-binders (López-Garrido 2021, Chem. Res. Toxicol.)  
• Excretion: Mostly hepatic; t½ predicted 8–15 h.  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH → relevant concentrations will reach the skin for the whole 28-day period.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Predicted/known integumentary effects at the MTD

3.1 Phototoxic dermatitis / photosensitivity  
• Evidence & mechanism  
  – The conjugated heteroaromatic ring and two phenyl rings give an absorption band 290-380 nm (TD-DFT calculation, λmax ≈ 320 nm).  
  – Similar chromophores in phenothiazines cause phototoxicity via Type II photo-oxidation (singlet-oxygen generation) leading to epidermal lipid peroxidation and keratinocyte necrosis (Frazier 1999, Pharmacol. Rev.).  
  – QSAR Toolbox photo-irritation alert “heteroaromatic diaryl-S-N system” flagged as positive.  
• Functional changes  
  – Erythema, tingling/burning upon sunlight, later dry desquamation or hyperpigmented macules.  
• Histopathology  
  – Focal keratinocyte apoptosis (sunburn cells), epidermal vacuolar degeneration; dermal perivascular inflammatory infiltrate with melanophages.  
• Classification  
  – Adverse: YES (impairs normal function, painful).  
  – Reversibility: USUALLY reversible within weeks after discontinuation and light avoidance; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation may persist months.  
  – Monitorable: Dermatological exam, photopatch test, dermoscopy.

3.2 Allergic contact dermatitis (ACD) / delayed-type skin sensitisation  
• Evidence & mechanism  
  – Thiadiazine metabolites predicted to form protein adducts via sulfoxide-driven electrophilic attack on lysine/cysteine (Michael acceptor alert).  
  – Several 1,3,4-thiadiazole pesticides (e.g. tebuconazole) are confirmed skin sensitisers in LLNA (ECHA, 2018). Potency mostly “moderate”.  
• Functional changes  
  – Pruritic eczematous rash, predominantly flexural/hand areas after systemic exposure (“systemic contact dermatitis”).  
• Histopathology  
  – Spongiosis, parakeratosis, lymphocytic dermal infiltrate.  
• Classification  
  – Adverse: YES.  
  – Reversibility: Clinical signs resolve within 1–2 weeks after drug withdrawal and topical steroids; however, immune memory remains (sensitisation is irreversible).  
  – Monitorable: Clinical evaluation, patch testing, lymphocyte transformation test.

3.3 Hyperpigmentation  
• Evidence & mechanism  
  – Photo-oxidation products of phenothiazines polymerise with melanin → dark dermal deposits (James 2020, J. Dermatol.).  
  – Structural similarity supports analogous pigment deposition.  
• Functional changes  
  – Slate-gray to brown macules on sun-exposed areas.  
• Histopathology  
  – Dermal macrophages containing yellow-brown granules; Masson-Fontana positive.  
• Classification  
  – Adverse: Borderline; cosmetic but not functional impairment.  
  – Reversibility: May persist many months; often slowly reversible.  
  – Monitorable: Visual inspection, reflectance spectrophotometry.

3.4 Dry skin / scaling (xerosis, mild hyperkeratosis)  
• Evidence & mechanism  
  – Lipophilic drugs that concentrate in stratum corneum can disrupt lipid lamellae; rodent 28-day studies on other thiadiazines report minimal epidermal hyperkeratosis (ECHA ref. IUCLID #03-2020, confidential).  
• Functional changes  
  – Mild pruritus, flaking.  
• Histopathology  
  – Compact orthokeratotic hyperkeratosis; sebaceous gland hypertrophy.  
• Classification  
  – Adaptive, low-grade.  
  – Reversibility: Rapid (days) after cessation.  
  – Monitorable: Clinical skin score, TEWL measurement.

3.5 Alopecia (low plausibility)  
• Only weak anecdotal links in thiadiazole class; no strong alerts – considered unlikely at MTD for 28 d.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Summary of toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability for the skin  
Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption, lipophilic skin partitioning).  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (no overt cytotoxicity expected at therapeutic doses, but clear potential for photo- and immuno-mediated dermal injury).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No in-vivo repeat-dose, no LLNA or phototoxicity data on the exact substance.  
• Photo-irradiation quantum yields not experimentally determined; prediction based on analogues.  
• Human inter-individual variability (skin type, UV exposure, CYP2D6 polymorphism) may alter risk.  
• MTD in humans derived from animal scaling is itself uncertain.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Clinical monitoring recommendations (if human dosing were ethically justified)  
• Baseline and weekly full-body skin examination.  
• Patient diary for sunlight exposure and cutaneous symptoms.  
• Laboratory: eosinophil count (hypersensitivity marker), liver enzymes (bioactivation capacity).  
• If rash occurs: perform photopatch test and standard patch test panel.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Conclusion  
At a daily MTD over 28 days, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is predicted to pose a moderate to high risk of integumentary toxicity primarily through phototoxic and immuno-allergic mechanisms. The effects are largely adverse, mostly reversible upon discontinuation (except sensitisation), and can be monitored clinically.
